flagged.title: AnyCalib: On-Manifold Learning for Model-Agnostic Single-View Camera Calibration
flagged.evidence: Citation fully confirmed: arXiv:2503.12701, authors Javier Tirado-Garin & Javier Civera (I3A, University of Zaragoza), ICCV 2025 (also IEEE Xplore + ICCV virtual poster page). Directional claim confirmed: independent sources state AnyCalib reaches new SOTA on angular/reprojection error and outperforms GeoCalib and 3D foundation models. The GeoCalib baseline figures in the claim are independently corroborated: GeoCalib vFoV median error ~3.21 deg and vFoV AUC@1=17.4 on Stanford2D3D (claim says 3.23 deg / 17.4 -- 3.23 vs 3.21 is a ~0.02 deg rounding/version difference). However, the specific AnyCalib (pinhole) numbers (1.13 deg angular, 2.55 deg vFoV, vFoV AUC@1/5/10 = 21.2/46.8/64.6) could NOT be independently confirmed: all primary tables (arXiv PDF/HTML, IEEE Xplore PDF, semanticscholar, project page) were blocked by the egress proxy (403) and search snippets did not echo those exact values. Marking uncertain because the headline AnyCalib numbers are unverified, though metadata and the comparative claim hold.
flagged.correction: Could not verify the exact AnyCalib row (1.13/2.55/21.2/46.8/64.6) against the primary source; GeoCalib vFoV error appears to be 3.21 deg (claim states 3.23 deg). Recommend checking Stanford2D3D table in arXiv:2503.12701 directly.

flagged.title: Pose-Based Fall Detection System: Efficient Monitoring on Standard CPUs
flagged.evidence: The paper exists with correct metadata: Vinayak Mali & Saurabh Jaiswal, Dept. of AI, IIT Kharagpur, arXiv:2503.19501 (Mar 2025); pose backbone is MediaPipe (the truncated 'Med...' = MediaPipe, correct); it does run in real time on standard CPUs with no GPU (correct). HOWEVER the key empirical claim is wrong. The actual paper is evaluated on the GMDCSA-24 dataset (~130 video clips), NOT on URFD (University of Rzeszow) or MCFD (Multiple Cameras Fall Dataset). Its reported metrics are accuracy 86.86%, precision 85.23%, recall 93.75%, F1 93.17% (overall accuracy 91.54%) -- not the cited precision 88.8% / recall 94.1% / F1 91.4% / specificity 95.6%. Those exact URFD+MCFD figures (88.8/94.1/91.4/95.6) actually belong to a DIFFERENT paper (the 'PIFR' pose-angle method using YOLOv11 + SVM, 17 keypoints). Sources: arxiv.org/abs/2503.19501 and arxiv.org/html/2503.19501v1; GMDCSA-24 dataset paper (PMC11416611); PIFR paper (PMC12173392).
flagged.correction: Mali & Jaiswal (arXiv:2503.19501) evaluate on the GMDCSA-24 dataset, not URFD/MCFD, and report accuracy 86.86%, precision 85.23%, recall 93.75%, F1 93.17%. The cited URFD+MCFD figures (precision 88.8%, recall 94.1%, F1 91.4%, specificity 95.6%) are from a different paper (PIFR, YOLOv11+SVM) and have been misattributed.

flagged.title: YOLO26: Key Architectural Enhancements and Performance Benchmarking for Real-Time Object Detection
flagged.evidence: Title and arXiv id (2509.25164, 2025) are correct, and the numeric benchmarks check out: across five scales YOLO26 reaches 40.9-57.5 mAP(50-95) on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT FP16; YOLO26n 40.9 mAP / 2.4M params / 5.4 GFLOPs / 1.7 ms T4 and YOLO26x 57.5 mAP / 55.7M params / 193.9 GFLOPs (consistent with Ultralytics docs and arxiv.org/abs/2509.25164). HOWEVER the authorship is wrong: the citation says 'Khan et al.' but the paper is authored by Ranjan Sapkota, Rahul Harsha Cheppally, Ajay Sharda, Manoj Karkee (Cornell University / Kansas State University), per ADS (2025arXiv250925164S), Semantic Scholar, and HuggingFace papers/2509.25164. No author named Khan is on this paper.
flagged.correction: Authors are Ranjan Sapkota, Rahul Harsha Cheppally, Ajay Sharda, and Manoj Karkee (Cornell Univ. / Kansas State Univ.), NOT 'Khan et al.' The title, arXiv id, year, and all cited performance numbers are otherwise accurate.

domainDigest.topMethod: YOLOv10: Real-Time End-to-End Object Detection
domainDigest.headline: COCO val, 640x640, T4 GPU TensorRT FP16 (official repo table): YOLOv10-N 38.5% AP / 2.3M params / 6.7G FLOPs / 1.84 ms; -S 46.3% / 7.2M / 21.6G / 2.49 ms; -M 51.1% / 15.4M / 59.1G / 4.74 ms; -B 52.5% / 19.1M / 92.0G / 5.74 ms; -L 53.2% / 24.4M / 120.3G / 7.28 ms; -X 54.4% / 29.5M / 160.4G / 10.70 ms. Headline comparisons from the abstract: YOLOv10-S is 1.8x faster than RT-DETR-R18 at similar AP with 2.8x fewer params and FLOPs; YOLOv10-B has 46% less latency and 25% fewer parameters than YOLOv9-C at the same performance; YOLOv10-L/X outperform/match RT-DETR-R101 (54.3% AP, 259G FLOPs) with far fewer FLOPs (YOLOv10-X ~1.3x faster).
domainDigest.integration: In src/models/yolo_wrapper.py, add YOLOv10 as a selectable backbone: the constructor already does YOLO(model_name), so passing "yolov10n.pt"/"yolov10s.pt" works through Ultralytics with no architectural change. Add an end_to_end flag that, when a yolov10* model is detected, sets the predict call to bypass NMS (pass the model's end-to-end/no-NMS path) and skips applying self._iou_threshold, since the one-to-one head emits one box per object. Surface a --model yolov10n.pt option (and document the NMS-free mode) in scripts/run_webcam.py and scripts/run_stream.py, and add yolov10n/s/m to the export + latency sweep in scripts/benchmark.py and scripts/export_model.py so its ONNX/TensorRT numbers are compared head-to-head against the current yolov8n/yolo11n defaults. This gives a near-constant-latency, post-processing-free option on edge hardware with a one-line model swap.

domainDigest.topMethod: Observation-Centric SORT: Rethinking SORT for Robust Multi-Object Tracking
domainDigest.headline: Official repo tables (private detections): MOT17-test HOTA 63.2 / MOTA 78.0 / IDF1 77.5; MOT20-test HOTA 62.4 / MOTA 75.9 / IDF1 76.4; DanceTrack-test HOTA 55.1 / MOTA 89.4 / IDF1 54.2 (AssA 38.0); KITTI-cars HOTA 76.5 / MOTA 90.3. The paper abstract reports a stronger DanceTrack HOTA of 62.1. Speed: OC-SORT association runs at ~700 FPS on a single i9 3.0GHz CPU when fed off-the-shelf detections (association cost only; end-to-end speed is dominated by the detector). The tracker is appearance-free, so it adds essentially zero extra model weights/memory on top of the detector.
domainDigest.integration: In src/tracking/tracker.py the EnhancedTracker currently wraps Ultralytics BoT-SORT/ByteTrack and bolts on a hand-rolled color-histogram re-ID. Switch the underlying tracker to OC-SORT, which recent Ultralytics ships natively: pass tracker="ocsort.yaml" (or "deepocsort.yaml") to model.track(...) wherever the detector is driven, and expose a tracker_type config option ('botsort'|'bytetrack'|'ocsort'|'deepocsort'). For an Ultralytics-independent, detector-agnostic path, add a thin backend using BoxMOT: `from boxmot import OCSORT; tracker = OCSORT(); tracks = tracker.update(dets, frame)` where dets is Nx6 (x1,y1,x2,y2,conf,cls). OC-SORT's built-in ORU virtual-trajectory recovery largely supersedes the brittle color-histogram match_reid()/lost-track logic in this file, so that custom re-ID code can be retired or kept only as an optional overlay; keep TrackHistory for trajectory visualization and stats.

domainDigest.topMethod: Omni-Scale Feature Learning for Person Re-Identification
domainDigest.headline: OSNet (osnet_x1_0) ~2.2M parameters, ~978M mult-adds -- roughly 1/10th of ResNet-50 (~25M params). Paper-reported single-query, no re-ranking: Market-1501 84.9% mAP / 94.8% rank-1; DukeMTMC-reID 73.5% mAP; MSMT17 52.9% mAP; CUHK03 also evaluated (six datasets total). Scaled-down osnet_x0_25 has only ~0.2M params yet ~77.8% mAP / 92.2% rank-1 on Market-1501. Note: official torchreid model-zoo softmax-only checkpoint reports a slightly lower 82.6% mAP / 94.2% rank-1 on Market-1501 (the deployable weights). Outputs a compact embedding (512-d in torchreid's feature_extractor).
domainDigest.integration: In src/tracking/tracker.py, EnhancedTracker._extract_embedding currently builds only a 96-d color histogram (cv2.calcHist over 3 channels x 32 bins) -- a weak, illumination-sensitive descriptor. Replace it with an OSNet appearance embedding: add a deep-person-reid (torchreid) FeatureExtractor loading osnet_x1_0 (or osnet_x0_25 for edge), resize each person crop to 256x128, and return the L2-normalized ~512-d vector instead of the histogram. The existing match_reid cosine-similarity logic and _embeddings dict need no structural change (just bump reid_threshold for the new feature space). Gate it to class_name == 'person', batch crops per frame, and fall back to the histogram if torch/torchreid is unavailable. This upgrades the re-entry re-ID and the BoT-SORT/ByteTrack appearance matching the module wraps, and slots naturally into the repo's ONNX/TensorRT export + benchmarking path.

domainDigest.topMethod: MCBLT: Multi-Camera Multi-Object 3D Tracking in Long Videos (originally 'BEV-SUSHI: Multi-Target Multi-Camera 3D Detection and Tracking in Bird's-Eye View')
domainDigest.headline: AI City'24 MTMC: 81.22 HOTA, state-of-the-art, with +9.28 HOTA, +2.91 DetA, +4.38 AssA, +1.85 LocA over the prior best. WILDTRACK: 95.6 IDF1, 92.6 MOTA, 93.7 MOTP, beating EarlyBird by +3.3 IDF1 and +3.1 MOTA using the same MVDet/ResNet-18 detections. Detector backbone ResNet-101. Speed: about 1.5 FPS end-to-end on a single NVIDIA A100 with 10 cameras, before any optimization. Scales to full-sequence association (up to roughly 4k frames) vs SUSHI's 512.
domainDigest.integration: Upgrade src/multicam/manager.py: the current find_cross_camera_matches only compares class_id and confidence (no geometry), so it cannot establish true cross-camera identity. Add a BEV fusion path. (1) Add per-camera homography config (4+ image<->ground-plane point pairs) loaded in MultiCameraManager / CameraFeed; compute H with cv2.findHomography once at setup. (2) New method project_to_bev(detections) that maps each detection's foot-point (bbox bottom-center) to common ground-plane (x,y) via cv2.perspectiveTransform. (3) Replace find_cross_camera_matches with a fuse_bev step doing Hungarian matching (scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment) on BEV Euclidean distance, optionally gated by ReID embeddings already used in src/tracking, to assign one global ID per physical object. (4) Render a top-down BEV map alongside the grid in create_grid, and expose --bev / --calib flags plus a calibration helper in scripts/run_multicam.py. This gives geometry-grounded cross-camera IDs at real-time speed without MCBLT's heavy 3D detector.

domainDigest.topMethod: Slicing Aided Hyper Inference and Fine-tuning for Small Object Detection
domainDigest.headline: Inference-only (no retraining) AP gains on VisDrone+xView: +6.8% FCOS, +5.1% VFNet, +5.3% TOOD. Adding slicing-aided fine-tuning gives cumulative gains of +12.7% (FCOS), +13.4% (VFNet), +14.5% (TOOD) AP. Cost scales with slice count: each patch is a separate forward pass, so a fixed grid is roughly Nx slower than a single full-frame pass (community measurements report ~10x end-to-end slowdown for dense grids, e.g. 12-15 slices, vs single-pass).
domainDigest.integration: Add a sliced-inference path to src/models/yolo_wrapper.py: add a `sahi: bool = False` (plus `slice_wh=(512,512)`, `overlap=0.2`) flag to YOLODetector.__init__, and in detect() branch to a `_detect_sliced()` that uses SAHI's get_sliced_prediction over the Ultralytics model (`AutoDetectionModel.from_pretrained(model_type='ultralytics', ...)`). Map the returned object-prediction list back into the existing Detection dataclass (bbox/confidence/class_id/class_name) so all downstream consumers — zone counting, line crossing, tracking/ReID, density heatmaps in src/analytics, and the Streamlit dashboard — work unchanged. Add `sahi` to requirements.txt, expose a 'Tiled / small-object mode' toggle plus a frame-stride control in src/dashboard.py, and keep it off by default in src/stream.py to preserve real-time FPS on full-frame mode.

domainDigest.topMethod: SAM 2: Segment Anything in Images and Videos
domainDigest.headline: ~44 FPS real-time video (A100). Variants (SAM 2.1, A100, torch 2.5.1): hiera_tiny 38.9M/91.2 FPS, small 46M/84.8, base_plus 80.8M/64.1, large 224.4M/39.5. Video J&F: SA-V test 76.5-79.5, MOSE val 71.8-74.6, LVOS v2 77.3-80.6. ~3x fewer interactions than prior video methods. Images: 6x faster AND more accurate than SAM (58.9 vs 58.1 mIoU 1-click; up to ~61.4 avg over 23 datasets; 5-click mIoU 81.7 over 37 datasets).
domainDigest.integration: Add src/models/sam2_segmenter.py (a Sam2Segmenter subclassing src/models/base.py) wrapping ultralytics SAM2 (sam2.1_t.pt). Auto-prompt with boxes from src/models/yolo_wrapper.py or text-driven boxes from src/models/open_vocab_detector.py (OWLv2), then stream masks frame-to-frame via SAM 2 memory so masks persist with track IDs from src/tracking. Upgrades zone counting/line crossing and privacy blurring (src/privacy.py) from boxes to pixel-accurate masks, and density heatmaps to mask-area. Surface it in src/dashboard.py as a 'Segment and track' mode; default GPU + sam2.1_t/s, fall back to click-to-segment-only on CPU.

domainDigest.topMethod: Depth Anything V2
domainDigest.headline: Model sizes: Small 24.8M, Base 97.5M, Large 335.3M, Giant 1.3B params. Training data: 595K synthetic labeled images (BlendedMVS, Hypersim, IRS, TartanAir, vKITTI2) + 62M pseudo-labeled real images (ImageNet, LSUN, BDD100K, OpenImages, SA-1B, etc.). Speed/accuracy: "over 10x faster" and more accurate than SD-based Marigold/GeoWizard. On the DA-2K relative-depth benchmark, ViT-S reaches 95.3% accuracy vs 86.8% for Marigold. Reported inference: small model under ~10 ms/image on A100 (PyTorch), and ~98.6 ms TensorRT for batch-4 480x300 on Jetson Orin NX (~50 ms on Orin AGX). Metric fine-tunes: max depth 20 m indoor (Hypersim), 80 m outdoor (Virtual KITTI 2).
domainDigest.integration: Add a depth subsystem mirroring the existing detector pattern. Create src/models/depth_estimator.py with a DepthEstimator class subclassing a new BaseDepthEstimator ABC (parallel to BaseDetector in src/models/base.py), wrapping Depth Anything V2-Small via ONNX Runtime (CUDA/OpenVINO EP) with a depth(frame)->np.ndarray method returning a normalized inverse-depth map. Add scripts/run_depth.py as a webcam demo mirroring the existing run_webcam.py, showing side-by-side RGB + INFERNO colormap. Then wire per-detection depth sampling: for each YOLO Detection, sample masked-median depth over a 20%-shrunk bbox with MAD outlier rejection, storing it on the Detection dataclass (extend src/models/base.py with an optional depth field). This unlocks depth-aware zone counting (3D prisms), too-close anomaly alerts, and a depth cost term in the BoT-SORT/ByteTrack association. Update docs/webcam_depth_research.md (already scaffolded) with the V2 benchmark numbers, the ONNX/OpenVINO INT8 recipe, and the metric-calibration plan; log experiments per its Section 8 milestones.

domainDigest.topMethod: REACT: Real-time Efficiency and Accuracy Compromise for Tradeoffs in Scene Graph Generation
domainDigest.headline: REACT achieves the highest inference speed among existing SGG models: 2.7x faster than SOTA while improving object-detection accuracy by 58%. Reported latency is 32.5ms (YOLOv8m configuration), with a real-time configuration reaching ~23ms; Dynamic Candidate Selection (DCS) reduces latency a further 66.5% on average without performance loss. Swapping Faster-RCNN for YOLOv8 gives an 84.99% latency improvement (~2.75x) and +19.4% F1@K. mean Recall is ~mR@K 18.0 (vs 20.7 for the follow-up REACT++). By contrast the prior one-stage transformer RelTR runs at 16.6 FPS with 63.7M params (R@50 27.5, mR@50 10.8). The follow-up REACT++ reaches 35.8M params and 13.4ms (~55 FPS) via ONNX export.
domainDigest.integration: Add a RelationGraphGenerator to src/analytics/scene_understanding.py that upgrades the current heuristic geometric relations (near/above/inside, computed in _find_relations) into a learned scene graph. Reuse the project's existing ultralytics YOLO detections as REACT's detection stage, then attach REACT's lightweight predicate head (load the MIT-licensed maelic/SGG-Benchmark PSG/VG150 ONNX weights via onnxruntime, matching the repo's existing ONNX export path) to classify semantic predicates (holding, riding, sitting-on) over the top-K filtered detection pairs. Populate the existing ObjectRelation.relation field with the predicted predicate (keep geometric relations as a fast fallback when no GPU/weights), feed the resulting triplets into the Streamlit dashboard and the alert system (e.g. trigger on person-holding-knife), and gate it behind a config flag so the CPU heuristic path stays the default.

domainDigest.topMethod: AnyCalib: On-Manifold Learning for Model-Agnostic Single-View Camera Calibration
domainDigest.headline: On Stanford2D3D, AnyCalib (pinhole) reaches 1.13 deg angular error, 2.55 deg vertical-FoV error and vFoV AUC@1/5/10 = 21.2/46.8/64.6, beating GeoCalib (1.45 deg angle, 3.23 deg vFoV, vFoV AUC@1=17.4) and 3D foundation models (DUSt3R, MoGe). The general variant anycalib_gen stays close at 1.30 deg / 2.95 deg vFoV while also handling distorted/fisheye inputs. Authors report consistently outperforming GeoCalib, WildCamera and DroidCalib across Stanford2D3D, MegaDepth, TartanAir and LaMAR despite training on orders of magnitude less data. Compute ~187.8 GMACs; backbone DINOv2 ViT-L/14 (~300M params). No FPS/latency table is published; calibration is a one-shot per-camera step, not per-frame.
domainDigest.integration: Add src/utils/calibration.py wrapping AnyCalib: a CameraCalibrator.calibrate(frame, cam_model='pinhole'|'radial:2'|'kb:4') returning intrinsics K + distortion coeffs, cached to a per-source JSON sidecar. Wire it into src/multicam/manager.py: add optional intrinsics fields to CameraFeed, run a lazy one-shot calibrate() per camera in MultiCameraManager.start() (GPU once), and apply cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap once + cv2.remap in read_all() so all downstream consumers get undistorted frames. Biggest payoff: replace the heuristic class/confidence matching in find_cross_camera_matches() with geometry-aware association by back-projecting detection centers through each camera's calibrated rays onto a common ground plane (homography), turning guesswork fusion into metric cross-camera matching. Calibrated intrinsics also upgrade src/analytics density heatmaps and zone/line counting to real-world units.

domainDigest.topMethod: Channel-wise Topology Refinement Graph Convolution for Skeleton-Based Action Recognition
domainDigest.headline: 4-stream ensemble accuracy: NTU RGB+D 60 = 92.4% (X-Sub) / 96.8% (X-View); NTU RGB+D 120 = 88.9% (X-Sub) / 90.6% (X-Set); state-of-the-art on NW-UCLA. Single CTR-GC model is very small: ~1.46M parameters and ~1.97 GFLOPs (vs ST-GC 1.22M/~1.65G), and in the ablation a single joint stream reaches 84.9% on NTU120 X-Sub. A third-party latency comparison reports CTR-GCN ~22.84 ms per inference (single sample), i.e. ~40+ FPS on a modern GPU.
domainDigest.integration: Replace the brittle rule-based heuristics in src/models/pose_detector.py (the ActionClassifier class, lines ~187-327) with a learned CTR-GCN head. Concretely: (1) add src/models/ctrgcn.py wrapping a single-stream CTR-GC network (port the official Uason-Chen/CTR-GCN model or use the PYSKL/MMAction2 implementation, exported to ONNX for opencv/onnxruntime inference consistent with src/deployment/exporter.py). (2) In src/analytics/temporal.py, add a per-track keypoint ring buffer (reuse TemporalBuffer/track_id plumbing) that accumulates ~64 frames of normalized COCO-17 keypoints from PoseResult.keypoints. (3) When a window fills, feed it to the GCN and write the predicted action + confidence back into PoseResult.action/action_confidence, so scripts/run_action.py's existing draw/label/summary loop works unchanged. (4) Extend scripts/run_action.py with a --classifier {rules,ctrgcn} flag and a --window-size arg; keep the rules path as fallback. Map the model's NTU label set down to the repo's action vocabulary (or expose full NTU classes). This upgrades the platform from geometric guesses to a real SOTA temporal action model while staying within its YOLO-pose + ONNX/TensorRT stack.

domainDigest.topMethod: Learning Memory-Guided Normality for Anomaly Detection
domainDigest.headline: Frame-level AUC, prediction variant: UCSD Ped2 97.0%, CUHK Avenue 88.5%, ShanghaiTech 70.5%. Reconstruction variant: Ped2 90.2%, Avenue 82.8%, ShanghaiTech 69.8%. The memory module adds roughly +2.7% to +4.0% AUC over the no-memory baseline. Speed: ~67 fps (real-time), faster than optical-flow-based competitors. Trained on a single Nvidia GTX TITAN Xp (~1 h / 15 h / 36 h for Ped2 / Avenue / ShanghaiTech). Built on a compact CNN encoder-decoder plus a small memory bank (a fraction of typical autoencoder size); exact parameter/memory footprint not stated in sources fetched.
domainDigest.integration: Add a second, pixel-level anomaly backend alongside the existing detection-metadata IsolationForest. Create src/analytics/mnad_anomaly.py implementing an MNADAnomalyDetector with the same update()/fit()/check() interface as src/analytics/anomaly_detector.py (so the rest of the app and the Streamlit dashboard are unchanged), wrapping the MNAD prediction encoder-decoder + memory module in torch. Add scripts/train_mnad.py to learn normal patterns from a short clip per source and save weights + memory items, and extend scripts/run_anomaly.py with a --backend {isoforest,mnad} flag plus --mnad-weights. Reuse src/deployment for ONNX/TensorRT export and src/alerts.py to fire on a smoothed PSNR-plus-memory-distance score above a per-camera threshold. This upgrades anomaly detection from "unusual object statistics" to true unsupervised motion/appearance anomalies, while keeping IsolationForest as the zero-training default.

domainDigest.topMethod: MiniROAD: Minimal RNN Framework for Online Action Detection
domainDigest.headline: THUMOS'14: 71.8% mAP (Kinetics two-stream I3D features), matching or beating the best transformer OAD methods; TVSeries: 89.6% mcAP; FineAction: 37.1% mAP (numbers from the official jbistanbul/MiniROAD repo). Efficiency: ~10.5M parameters and ~1.08 GFLOPs vs a comparable transformer baseline at ~12.9M params / ~1.96G (roughly half the compute). The GRU itself runs about 200x faster than prior OAD methods (per-frame model latency) on a single RTX 3090; with optical-flow + I3D feature extraction included, end-to-end "Overall FPS" is much lower and is the real bottleneck.
domainDigest.integration: Add a learned streaming action head to src/analytics/temporal.py. Today that file is purely heuristic/geometric (loitering, abandoned-object, crowd, speed via pixel displacement and durations) with no learned temporal model. Implement a MiniRoadHead class wrapping a single-layer GRU (hidden 1024) that persists its hidden state across frames, mirroring the existing per-frame TemporalBuffer.add() / EventDetector.check() loop. Per frame, extract a lightweight RGB clip embedding (reuse the existing torch backbone or a small X3D/MobileNet), GRU-step it, and emit current-action logits; surface high-confidence transitions as a new EventType.ACTION_RECOGNIZED TemporalEvent so they flow into the existing alert system and Streamlit dashboard. Ship a small trainer in src/training/ that applies MiniROAD's non-uniform per-timestep loss weighting, keeping flow optional/off by default for real-time webcam use.

domainDigest.topMethod: Rethinking Counting and Localization in Crowds: A Purely Point-Based Framework
domainDigest.headline: ShanghaiTech Part A MAE ~52.7 / MSE ~85.1; Part B MAE ~6.25 / MSE ~9.9 (best at publication, ~4.8% MAE and ~12.9% MSE reduction vs prior best ADSCNet). UCF-QNRF MAE ~85.3; also strong on UCF-CC-50 and NWPU-Crowd. Backbone VGG16-bn, ~21.8M parameters. Reported real-time on GPU: ~94.5 FPS (~21.8 ms/frame) in third-party efficiency comparisons, far above VGG-density methods (~23-28 FPS) and HRNet-based STEERER (~17 FPS). Localization measured with the new nAP metric (per-dataset nAP roughly 50-65% depending on threshold).
domainDigest.integration: Add a crowd-counting model that emits point predictions and reuse the existing analytics seam. Create src/models/p2pnet_wrapper.py wrapping the official VGG16-bn P2PNet weights and returning the repo's Detection objects with zero-area boxes centered on each head point plus a 'crowd' class and confidence. Add scripts/run_crowd_counter.py mirroring scripts/run_heatmap.py: stream frames, run P2PNet, overlay the live head count, and feed the point Detections straight into the existing HeatmapGenerator (src/analytics/heatmap.py — it already accumulates detection centers) so dense-crowd heatmaps stop undercounting where YOLO fails. The same point Detections also drop into src/analytics/zone_counter.py for true per-zone occupancy in congested areas, and into the alert system (src/alerts.py) for crowd-density thresholds. Wire an ONNX/TensorRT export path via scripts/export_model.py + scripts/benchmark.py for edge throughput.

domainDigest.topMethod: XFeat: Accelerated Features for Lightweight Image Matching
domainDigest.headline: Speed (Intel i5-1135G7 laptop CPU, VGA/640x480): sparse XFeat 27.1 FPS, semi-dense XFeat* 19.2 FPS, vs SuperPoint 3.0 FPS and DISK 1.2 FPS — about 9x faster than SuperPoint, and comparable accuracy to DISK at ~16x its speed; up to ~5x faster than competing deep local features overall. GPU: ~1,400 FPS sparse VGA on an RTX 4090, and easily >150 FPS single-batch VGA. Accuracy (relative pose, MegaDepth-1500): AUC@5/10/20 ≈ 42.6 / 56.4 / 67.7, comparable to SuperPoint while much cheaper; it also generalizes to indoor ScanNet-1500 without being trained on it. Compact 64-D descriptors; small enough for embedded deployment.
domainDigest.integration: Add src/utils/features.py wrapping XFeat (load via torch.hub 'verlab/accelerated_features'; detectAndCompute -> keypoints + 64-D descriptors; match() doing mutual-NN + cv2.findHomography RANSAC) as an OpenCV-style alternative to ORB/SIFT. Wire it into two existing weak spots: (1) src/multicam/manager.py find_cross_camera_matches — replace the class-id + confidence-difference heuristic with real geometric correspondence (estimate an inter-camera homography/fundamental matrix from XFeat matches to confirm two detections are the same physical object across overlapping views); (2) src/tracking/tracker.py _extract_embedding — augment the color-histogram re-ID with XFeat keypoint matching between a track's stored crop and re-entry candidates for far more robust re-identification. It also enables a new src/utils/stabilize.py computing the homography between consecutive src/stream.py frames for camera-motion compensation, stabilizing density heatmaps and cutting false anomaly/line-crossing triggers on shaky feeds.

domainDigest.topMethod: Pose-Based Fall Detection System: Efficient Monitoring on Standard CPUs
domainDigest.headline: On the University of Rzeszow Fall Detection (URFD) dataset and the Multiple Cameras Fall Dataset (MCFD): precision 88.8%, recall 94.1%, F1-score 91.4%, specificity 95.6%. Runs in real time on standard CPUs (no GPU); the pose backbone is MediaPipe Pose / BlazePose (lightweight, few-MB on-device model). The paper emphasizes CPU real-time operation but does not publish an explicit FPS/latency figure or a measured parameter/memory count.
domainDigest.integration: The repo's src/models/pose_detector.py ActionClassifier already mirrors this paper but in a weaker form: it uses a single crude torso-angle<30 degrees threshold over a 15-frame deque to flag "falling". Upgrade it to the paper's multi-feature + voting scheme: in ActionClassifier add a FallDetector that, per track_id, computes the paper's six features (height/aspect ratio, torso-leg angle, knee-ankle position, head-floor distance from frame-bottom, upper-body alignment, hip movement speed), thresholds each, and accumulates votes over a 20-frame buffer (raise sequence_length 15->20) before emitting a high-confidence "fall" only when the vote count persists. Wire the confirmed fall into src/alerts.py (emit a fall alert with track_id, bbox, and feature snapshot) so it flows into the existing alert system / Streamlit dashboard. This adds debounced, multi-cue fall detection with near-zero added compute on the current pose pipeline.

domainDigest.topMethod: YOLO26: Key Architectural Enhancements and Performance Benchmarking for Real-Time Object Detection
domainDigest.headline: Across five detection scales YOLO26 reaches 40.9-57.5 mAP(50-95) on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT(FP16) latency. YOLO26n: ~40.9 mAP, 2.4M params, 5.4B FLOPs, 38.9 ms CPU ONNX, 1.7 ms T4; YOLO26x: 57.5 mAP, 55.7M params, 193.9B FLOPs, ~525.8 ms CPU ONNX, 11.8 ms T4. Mid/large scales: YOLO26m above ~53% and YOLO26l above ~55% mAP at lower latency than transformer detectors. Reports up to 43% faster CPU ONNX inference for YOLO26n vs YOLO11n on an Intel Xeon CPU @ 2.00 GHz. Critically for edge: YOLO26 stays stable under INT8 quantization, whereas RT-DETRv3 shows sharp INT8 accuracy drops.
domainDigest.integration: Adopt YOLO26 as a first-class backbone in the existing export/benchmark pipeline. (1) In src/deployment/exporter.py, change the default model from "yolov8n.pt" to "yolo26n.pt" (or add a model-family arg) — the ModelExporter.export() path already calls ultralytics YOLO(...).export(format=...), so NMS-free/DFL-free YOLO26 exports cleanly to onnx/engine/openvino/coreml/tflite/ncnn with no code change. (2) Add an INT8 quantization path: extend ModelExporter.export() with an int8: bool flag passing int8=True (with a calibration data arg) for openvino/tflite/engine, and have scripts/export_model.py expose --int8 — this directly exploits YOLO26's quantization stability. (3) In scripts/benchmark.py / BenchmarkRunner.compare(), add a head-to-head matrix (YOLO26n FP32 vs FP16 vs INT8 vs YOLO11n/YOLOv8n) on CPU(ONNX) and GPU(TensorRT) so the repo can empirically confirm the "~43% faster CPU" and INT8-stability claims on the target hardware before swapping the default detector used by scripts/run_webcam.py.

domainDigest.topMethod: Plug and Play Active Learning for Object Detection
domainDigest.headline: Benchmarked on MS-COCO and Pascal VOC with RetinaNet and Faster R-CNN. In the early active-learning rounds (rounds <=3) PPAL outperforms the previous SOTA MI-AOD/MIAL by 3.4 AP on MS-COCO and 2.8 mAP on Pascal VOC, and outperforms prior work by a large margin overall. Setup: COCO initial labeled set = 2% of images, Pascal VOC initial set = 5%. (Context for the regime: on Pascal VOC, MI-AOD reaches ~72.27% mAP using only 20% of labels, ~93% of the 100%-data performance; PPAL pushes this curve higher.) No FPS/latency/params reported -- it is a data-selection algorithm, not a new detector, so it inherits the speed of whatever detector you plug in.
domainDigest.integration: The repo's src/training/active_learner.py currently does pure heuristic uncertainty (UncertaintySampler._compute_uncertainty, lines 98-155: confidence-band + class-conflict + frame-edge scores) with NO diversity step and NO class-difficulty calibration -- so it queues redundant near-duplicate frames. Concretely: (1) Add difficulty calibration to _compute_uncertainty -- track a per-class running difficulty coefficient (e.g. from rolling mean confidence + box-stability/IoU jitter across frames via the existing tracker) and multiply each instance's uncertainty by it, so rare/hard classes survive ranking. (2) Add a new diversity pass, e.g. a select_diverse_batch() method (or a src/training/diversity_sampler.py) that, at export_for_labeling() time, embeds each queued frame (mean of detection crop features, or a lightweight backbone) and runs scikit-learn KMeans++ (sklearn.cluster.kmeans_plusplus -- already a project dep) to pick one representative per cluster -- a practical CCMS stand-in. (3) Surface a scripts/run_active_learning.py flag like --strategy ppal --query-size 100 to run uncertainty-shortlist then diversity-select, replacing the current "top-N by score" behavior, and report the chosen frames to the Streamlit dashboard.

domainDigest.topMethod: ODGEN: Domain-specific Object Detection Data Generation with Diffusion Models
domainDigest.headline: Adding ODGEN synthetic data improves up to +25.3% mAP@.50:.95 on YOLOv5/YOLOv7 across 7 domain-specific Roboflow-100 subsets, the largest gain among compared generative methods (GLIGEN, ControlNet, etc.). On COCO-2014 (general domain) it leads prior methods by up to +5.6% mAP@.50:.95. Protocol: baselines use 200 real images; augmented runs add 5,000 synthetic images (a from-scratch variant trains YOLO on 10k synthetic, validates on 31k real). Built on Stable Diffusion with a fine-tuned UNet. No real-time/FPS numbers are reported (it is an offline data generator, not an inference model); the follow-up FLORA paper notes ODGEN's pipeline assumes enterprise GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA V100) and generation of thousands of samples.
domainDigest.integration: src/training/augmentation.py is misnamed today: its SyntheticGenerator only does classical geometric/photometric/mosaic/cutout transforms. Add a real diffusion backend, e.g. a DiffusionSyntheticGenerator class (or src/training/diffusion_augment.py) wrapping HuggingFace diffusers Stable Diffusion plus a bbox/ControlNet-style spatial condition, implementing ODGEN's two ideas: (1) fine-tune the UNet on cropped foreground crops + full frames captured from the user's own footage, (2) generate images conditioned on synthesized box layouts with per-box class text, then run a foreground/background discriminator filter before writing labels. Reuse the existing export_dataset() YOLO/dataset.yaml writer so output drops straight into the ultralytics training path. Wire the trigger to src/training/active_learner.py: classes/scenes it surfaces as low-confidence become the prompts/boxes ODGEN generates for, closing an offline rare-class data-flywheel that feeds the real-time detector.

domainDigest.topMethod: DeepPrivacy2: Towards Realistic Full-Body Anonymization
domainDigest.headline: FID 0.56 on FDF (128x128), improving on DeepPrivacy1 (FID 0.68). Datasets introduced: FDF256 (242,031 train / 6,533 val images, 256x256, from YFCC100M) and FDH (Flickr Diverse Humans, ~1.5M full-body images). Speed: ~11.6 FPS for the CSE-guided full-body generator and ~7.9 FPS for the face generator on a single NVIDIA GTX 1080 (8GB); a comparison SG-GAN generator ran ~7.3 FPS. Reported to give the lowest pose-estimation endpoint error (EPE) among anonymization baselines and better body/face pose retention than blurring (though noted as unsuitable for fine hand-pose work).
domainDigest.integration: Add a RealisticAnonymizer class to src/privacy.py alongside the existing PrivacyFilter, wrapping DeepPrivacy2 (pip install of the hukkelas/deep_privacy2 toolbox) behind the same apply(frame, detections) interface so it is a drop-in upgrade from reversible blur to GAN replacement. Drive it from a new mode (e.g. mode="synthesize") and feed it the person/face bboxes the YOLO detector already produces. Crucially, gate GAN calls through the existing multi-object tracker + re-ID so each identity is synthesized once and cached per track rather than every frame, recovering most of the FPS. Expose it in scripts/run_privacy.py via a --mode synthesize flag and a --device cuda guard that warns/falls back to blur when no GPU is present, and add a DeepPrivacy2 row to scripts/benchmark.py to measure on-device FPS before enabling it live.

domainDigest.topMethod: DINOv3
domainDigest.headline: ViT-7B frozen backbone (no fine-tuning): ImageNet-1k linear-probe ~88.4% top-1; ADE20K semantic seg 55.9 mIoU (~+6 over DINOv2); NYUv2 depth RMSE 0.309 (best reported); COCO detection 66.1 mAP (frozen backbone + Plain-DETR, SOTA). Pretrained on LVD-1689M (~1.689B images). Distilled: ViT-S 21M, ViT-S+ 29M, ViT-B 86M, ViT-L 300M, ViT-H+ 840M; ConvNeXt-T/S/B/L 29M-198M. Proxy latency: DINOv2 ViT-B ~10-12 ms/img @224 in TensorRT FP16 (~80-100 FPS).
domainDigest.integration: src/models/base.py today only defines BaseDetector (detect / detect_and_track). Add a sibling FeatureExtractor ABC there (embed(frame)->np.ndarray global vector; embed_dense(frame)->grid of patch features), then implement DINOv3Backbone (e.g. src/models/dinov3_backbone.py) loading facebook/dinov3-vits16 via transformers with a frozen forward. Wire its embeddings into the existing re-identification (more robust tracking), scene anomaly detection (k-NN/Mahalanobis on frozen features), density heatmaps and scene understanding (dense patch features), and the active-learning sampler. Export the small variant through the repo's existing ONNX/TensorRT path for edge.

paper.title: YOLOv10: Real-Time End-to-End Object Detection
paper.authors: Wang, Chen, Liu et al. (Ao Wang, Hui Chen, Lihao Liu, Kai Chen, Zijia Lin, Jungong Han, Guiguang Ding)

paperCards.method: YOLOv10 has two pillars. (1) Consistent dual assignments for NMS-free training: the model keeps a one-to-many head (rich supervision, high recall) alongside a one-to-one head, with a consistent matching metric so both heads rank predictions identically. At inference only the one-to-one head runs, producing a single box per object with no NMS, removing post-processing latency and the IoU-threshold hyperparameter. (2) Holistic efficiency-accuracy driven model design: efficiency-oriented changes (lightweight classification head, spatial-channel decoupled downsampling, rank-guided block allocation) cut redundant compute, while accuracy-oriented changes (large-kernel convolutions, partial self-attention/PSA) add capability cheaply. Together these reshape the accuracy-latency Pareto front. Built on the Ultralytics framework, models ship as N/S/M/B/L/X scales.
paperCards.headlineResults: COCO val, 640x640, T4 GPU TensorRT FP16 (official repo table): YOLOv10-N 38.5% AP / 2.3M params / 6.7G FLOPs / 1.84 ms; -S 46.3% / 7.2M / 21.6G / 2.49 ms; -M 51.1% / 15.4M / 59.1G / 4.74 ms; -B 52.5% / 19.1M / 92.0G / 5.74 ms; -L 53.2% / 24.4M / 120.3G / 7.28 ms; -X 54.4% / 29.5M / 160.4G / 10.70 ms. Headline comparisons from the abstract: YOLOv10-S is 1.8x faster than RT-DETR-R18 at similar AP with 2.8x fewer params and FLOPs; YOLOv10-B has 46% less latency and 25% fewer parameters than YOLOv9-C at the same performance; YOLOv10-L/X outperform/match RT-DETR-R101 (54.3% AP, 259G FLOPs) with far fewer FLOPs (YOLOv10-X ~1.3x faster).
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit. YOLOv10-N (2.3M params, 6.7G FLOPs) and -S (7.2M, 21.6G) are tiny and run end-to-end with no NMS, so per-frame post-processing is removed entirely. On a modern consumer GPU (RTX-class), N/S easily exceed real-time at 640px (the T4 reference is 1.84/2.49 ms, i.e. ~400-500 FPS with TensorRT; PyTorch FP16 is slower but still well above 30 FPS). On CPU, expect roughly 10-30+ FPS for the N model at 640px depending on cores and ONNX/OpenVINO backend; drop input to 416-480px or use INT8 to hit 30 FPS on weaker edge boxes. For a live webcam stream: load yolov10n.pt via Ultralytics, run streaming inference, and rely on the one-to-one head (no IoU threshold tuning). The NMS-free path is the key edge win because it makes latency near-constant regardless of crowd density.
paperCards.integrationIdea: In src/models/yolo_wrapper.py, add YOLOv10 as a selectable backbone: the constructor already does YOLO(model_name), so passing "yolov10n.pt"/"yolov10s.pt" works through Ultralytics with no architectural change. Add an end_to_end flag that, when a yolov10* model is detected, sets the predict call to bypass NMS (pass the model's end-to-end/no-NMS path) and skips applying self._iou_threshold, since the one-to-one head emits one box per object. Surface a --model yolov10n.pt option (and document the NMS-free mode) in scripts/run_webcam.py and scripts/run_stream.py, and add yolov10n/s/m to the export + latency sweep in scripts/benchmark.py and scripts/export_model.py so its ONNX/TensorRT numbers are compared head-to-head against the current yolov8n/yolo11n defaults. This gives a near-constant-latency, post-processing-free option on edge hardware with a one-line model swap.
paperCards.limitations: The reported speeds are measured on an NVIDIA T4 with TensorRT FP16, not raw PyTorch or CPU, so they overstate throughput on consumer hardware. The NMS-free one-to-one head can incur a small AP drop versus an NMS one-to-many head at equal compute (partly offset by consistent dual-assignment training). PSA/large-kernel modules add some architectural complexity. Pretrained weights are COCO-80-class only (no open-vocabulary). The original THU-MIG repo and Ultralytics distribution are AGPL-3.0, constraining closed-source commercial use.

paper.title: Observation-Centric SORT: Rethinking SORT for Robust Multi-Object Tracking
paper.authors: Cao, Pang, Weng, Khirodkar, Kitani (CMU)

paperCards.method: OC-SORT keeps the SORT pipeline (detect, Kalman-predict, IoU-match) but makes association observation-centric via three training-free additions. ORU (Observation-centric Re-Update): when a lost track is re-associated, it builds a virtual trajectory by interpolating between the last and current real observations and re-runs the Kalman filter back along that path, cleaning the noise accumulated during the occlusion gap. OCM (Observation-Centric Momentum): adds a velocity-direction-consistency term to the cost matrix, computing motion direction from actual observations over a window rather than the noisy filter velocity, so matching respects real motion. OCR (Observation-Centric Recovery): a second association stage that matches still-unmatched tracks to detections using the last observed position, recovering objects after short disappearances. All three are detector-agnostic, add negligible compute, and need no appearance/ReID network.
paperCards.headlineResults: Official repo tables (private detections): MOT17-test HOTA 63.2 / MOTA 78.0 / IDF1 77.5; MOT20-test HOTA 62.4 / MOTA 75.9 / IDF1 76.4; DanceTrack-test HOTA 55.1 / MOTA 89.4 / IDF1 54.2 (AssA 38.0); KITTI-cars HOTA 76.5 / MOTA 90.3. The paper abstract reports a stronger DanceTrack HOTA of 62.1. Speed: OC-SORT association runs at ~700 FPS on a single i9 3.0GHz CPU when fed off-the-shelf detections (association cost only; end-to-end speed is dominated by the detector). The tracker is appearance-free, so it adds essentially zero extra model weights/memory on top of the detector.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Excellent fit for consumer webcams and edge devices. OC-SORT is a lightweight, appearance-free (no ReID CNN) post-processor: its association runs at hundreds of FPS even on a CPU, so on a live webcam stream the only real cost is the YOLO detector. With a small detector (e.g. yolo*n/yolo*s) on a consumer GPU or even a modern CPU/edge accelerator, the pipeline stays comfortably real-time (30+ FPS) and tracking adds <1-2 ms per frame. Because it needs no embedding model, it has a tiny memory/compute footprint versus Deep OC-SORT, StrongSORT, or SAM2MOT (the latter is explicitly not yet real-time). It is the natural drop-in to make this repo's tracking robust to occlusion and non-linear motion without spending FPS.
paperCards.integrationIdea: In src/tracking/tracker.py the EnhancedTracker currently wraps Ultralytics BoT-SORT/ByteTrack and bolts on a hand-rolled color-histogram re-ID. Switch the underlying tracker to OC-SORT, which recent Ultralytics ships natively: pass tracker="ocsort.yaml" (or "deepocsort.yaml") to model.track(...) wherever the detector is driven, and expose a tracker_type config option ('botsort'|'bytetrack'|'ocsort'|'deepocsort'). For an Ultralytics-independent, detector-agnostic path, add a thin backend using BoxMOT: `from boxmot import OCSORT; tracker = OCSORT(); tracks = tracker.update(dets, frame)` where dets is Nx6 (x1,y1,x2,y2,conf,cls). OC-SORT's built-in ORU virtual-trajectory recovery largely supersedes the brittle color-histogram match_reid()/lost-track logic in this file, so that custom re-ID code can be retired or kept only as an optional overlay; keep TrackHistory for trajectory visualization and stats.
paperCards.limitations: Reported numbers reuse other methods' detections and shared hyper-parameters; the authors note per-dataset tuning can do better, so out-of-the-box numbers are conservative. Pure motion association with no appearance cue means it can still swap IDs under long occlusions or among visually similar, densely crowded targets (lower AssA on DanceTrack vs appearance-based Deep OC-SORT). OCM's velocity-direction cue assumes a roughly stable frame; strong camera motion is not compensated (unlike BoT-SORT's GMC). The 700 FPS figure is association-only, not full detection+tracking.

paper.title: Omni-Scale Feature Learning for Person Re-Identification
paper.authors: Zhou, Yang, Cavallaro, Xiang

paperCards.method: OSNet (Omni-Scale Network) is a lightweight CNN built from a residual block that contains multiple parallel convolutional streams, each with a different receptive-field size (achieved by stacking Lite 3x3 layers) so each stream detects features at one scale. A novel unified aggregation gate (AG) -- a small subnetwork producing input-dependent, channel-wise weights shared across streams -- dynamically fuses these multi-scale feature maps into omni-scale features (dynamic combinations spanning small to large scales). To stay tiny and avoid overfitting on modest ReID datasets, every convolution is factorized into a pointwise (1x1) plus depthwise (3x3) "Lite" convolution. Blocks are stacked layer-by-layer; the network is trained from scratch on ReID benchmarks with softmax/cross-entropy (label smoothing). Inference yields a compact appearance embedding compared by cosine/Euclidean distance.
paperCards.headlineResults: OSNet (osnet_x1_0) ~2.2M parameters, ~978M mult-adds -- roughly 1/10th of ResNet-50 (~25M params). Paper-reported single-query, no re-ranking: Market-1501 84.9% mAP / 94.8% rank-1; DukeMTMC-reID 73.5% mAP; MSMT17 52.9% mAP; CUHK03 also evaluated (six datasets total). Scaled-down osnet_x0_25 has only ~0.2M params yet ~77.8% mAP / 92.2% rank-1 on Market-1501. Note: official torchreid model-zoo softmax-only checkpoint reports a slightly lower 82.6% mAP / 94.2% rank-1 on Market-1501 (the deployable weights). Outputs a compact embedding (512-d in torchreid's feature_extractor).
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit for consumer webcams/edge. At ~2.2M params (or ~0.2M for x0.25) it is one to two orders smaller than ViT-based TransReID/CLIP-ReID (~86M+), runs on both CPU and GPU, and exports to ONNX/OpenVINO/TFLite. Reported real-world YOLO11+OSNet pipelines hit ~20 FPS on resource-constrained edge devices; on a consumer GPU it is effectively free relative to the YOLO detector. Per-crop input is 256x128, embeddings (e.g. 512-d) are compared by cosine similarity. For a live webcam: crop each tracked person box, batch the crops through OSNet once per frame (or every N frames), and cache embeddings -- this keeps the added latency to a few ms on GPU and keeps the pipeline interactive. The x0.25/x0.5 variants are the lever for CPU-only or Jetson-class hardware.
paperCards.integrationIdea: In src/tracking/tracker.py, EnhancedTracker._extract_embedding currently builds only a 96-d color histogram (cv2.calcHist over 3 channels x 32 bins) -- a weak, illumination-sensitive descriptor. Replace it with an OSNet appearance embedding: add a deep-person-reid (torchreid) FeatureExtractor loading osnet_x1_0 (or osnet_x0_25 for edge), resize each person crop to 256x128, and return the L2-normalized ~512-d vector instead of the histogram. The existing match_reid cosine-similarity logic and _embeddings dict need no structural change (just bump reid_threshold for the new feature space). Gate it to class_name == 'person', batch crops per frame, and fall back to the histogram if torch/torchreid is unavailable. This upgrades the re-entry re-ID and the BoT-SORT/ByteTrack appearance matching the module wraps, and slots naturally into the repo's ONNX/TensorRT export + benchmarking path.
paperCards.limitations: Trained per-domain with supervision; cross-domain/generalization is weaker (the IBN/AIN variants, e.g. osnet_ain, were added later specifically to improve domain generalization). It is a feature extractor only -- it provides no detector, tracker, or temporal association, so it must be paired with YOLO detection + a tracker. Accuracy degrades on heavily occluded or partial bodies (occluded ReID is a separate sub-problem the transformer-based TransReID targets) and on very low-resolution crops. Embeddings are appearance-based, so similarly-dressed people (uniforms) confuse it. Standard benchmark numbers assume reasonably clean person crops.

paper.title: MCBLT: Multi-Camera Multi-Object 3D Tracking in Long Videos (originally 'BEV-SUSHI: Multi-Target Multi-Camera 3D Detection and Tracking in Bird's-Eye View')
paper.authors: Wang, Meinhardt, Cetintas, Yang, Pusegaonkar, Missaoui, Biswas, Tang, Leal-Taixe (NVIDIA / TU Munich / Univ. of Washington)

paperCards.method: Two stages. (1) 3D detection in BEV: each synchronized camera image goes through an image backbone (ResNet-101); a spatial encoder lifts and aggregates the multi-view features using camera intrinsics/extrinsics into one unified BEV feature map; a temporal encoder fuses BEV features over a short window; a DETR-style decoder regresses 3D boxes directly in BEV. (2) Tracking-by-detection in 3D: it adapts SUSHI (Cetintas et al., CVPR 2023), a hierarchical graph neural network that builds graphs over detections and learns associations over progressively longer time spans, to run on 3D/BEV detections, the first 3D hierarchical-GNN tracker. A new near-online global merging block replaces SUSHI's heuristic sliding-window stitching, associating tracks across the full sequence (vs SUSHI's ~512-frame limit) to bridge long occlusion gaps and partial camera coverage.
paperCards.headlineResults: AI City'24 MTMC: 81.22 HOTA, state-of-the-art, with +9.28 HOTA, +2.91 DetA, +4.38 AssA, +1.85 LocA over the prior best. WILDTRACK: 95.6 IDF1, 92.6 MOTA, 93.7 MOTP, beating EarlyBird by +3.3 IDF1 and +3.1 MOTA using the same MVDet/ResNet-18 detections. Detector backbone ResNet-101. Speed: about 1.5 FPS end-to-end on a single NVIDIA A100 with 10 cameras, before any optimization. Scales to full-sequence association (up to roughly 4k frames) vs SUSHI's 512.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Not directly runnable on a consumer webcam in real time as-is. The reported ~1.5 FPS is on a datacenter A100 with 10 cameras and ResNet-101 backbones plus a BEV spatio-temporal transformer and a GNN tracker, before optimization; a 2-4 camera consumer-GPU (or CPU) setup would be sub-1 FPS. It also fundamentally needs calibrated, synchronized, overlapping cameras, which a typical webcam rig lacks. The transferable, real-time-friendly idea is the architecture, not the weights: fuse per-camera 2D detections into a shared ground plane (BEV) via homography from a one-time calibration, then associate in that metric space. For interactive FPS, keep the existing YOLO 2D detector per stream, project detection foot-points to BEV with cv2.findHomography / perspectiveTransform, and do greedy or Hungarian association by BEV distance plus appearance, optionally a lightweight GNN only at low rate. That preserves real-time on edge while borrowing MCBLT's BEV-fusion principle.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Upgrade src/multicam/manager.py: the current find_cross_camera_matches only compares class_id and confidence (no geometry), so it cannot establish true cross-camera identity. Add a BEV fusion path. (1) Add per-camera homography config (4+ image<->ground-plane point pairs) loaded in MultiCameraManager / CameraFeed; compute H with cv2.findHomography once at setup. (2) New method project_to_bev(detections) that maps each detection's foot-point (bbox bottom-center) to common ground-plane (x,y) via cv2.perspectiveTransform. (3) Replace find_cross_camera_matches with a fuse_bev step doing Hungarian matching (scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment) on BEV Euclidean distance, optionally gated by ReID embeddings already used in src/tracking, to assign one global ID per physical object. (4) Render a top-down BEV map alongside the grid in create_grid, and expose --bev / --calib flags plus a calibration helper in scripts/run_multicam.py. This gives geometry-grounded cross-camera IDs at real-time speed without MCBLT's heavy 3D detector.
paperCards.limitations: Requires calibrated cameras (known intrinsics/extrinsics) and time-synchronized, overlapping multi-view coverage; not applicable to a single uncalibrated webcam or non-overlapping cameras. Far from real-time: ~1.5 FPS on an A100 with 10 cameras pre-optimization, so heavy compute (multi-view ResNet-101 backbones + BEV transformer + GNN). Near-online/offline rather than strictly causal online. Long occlusion gaps without full environment coverage remain hard. Evaluated mainly on indoor, largely synthetic AI City scenes plus small WILDTRACK; generalization to outdoor/dynamic rigs less tested. Depends on detection quality (WILDTRACK numbers reuse EarlyBird detections).

paper.title: Slicing Aided Hyper Inference and Fine-tuning for Small Object Detection
paper.authors: Akyon, Altinuc, Temizel

paperCards.method: SAHI has two parts. (1) Slicing Aided Hyper Inference, a model-agnostic, training-free wrapper: the image is cut into a grid of overlapping patches (e.g. 512x512 with ~0.2 overlap), each patch is upscaled and passed through the existing detector so small objects appear larger relative to the receptive field; optionally a full-image pass is added to retain large objects. Per-slice boxes are mapped back to original coordinates and merged via NMS/Greedy-NMM to remove duplicates at slice seams. (2) Slicing Aided Fine-tuning: the detector is additionally fine-tuned on sliced patches (augmented dataset) so its weights adapt to the magnified small-object distribution, compounding the inference-time gain. It plugs onto any detector (Detectron2, MMDetection, YOLOv5/Ultralytics) with no architecture change.
paperCards.headlineResults: Inference-only (no retraining) AP gains on VisDrone+xView: +6.8% FCOS, +5.1% VFNet, +5.3% TOOD. Adding slicing-aided fine-tuning gives cumulative gains of +12.7% (FCOS), +13.4% (VFNet), +14.5% (TOOD) AP. Cost scales with slice count: each patch is a separate forward pass, so a fixed grid is roughly Nx slower than a single full-frame pass (community measurements report ~10x end-to-end slowdown for dense grids, e.g. 12-15 slices, vs single-pass).
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Model-agnostic and adds zero params (it wraps the existing YOLO weights this repo already loads), so memory is unchanged — only throughput drops. The catch is the per-slice forward passes: a 1080p frame at 512px/0.2-overlap is ~12-15 slices, i.e. ~10x the per-frame cost. On a consumer GPU (e.g. RTX 3060/T4) a YOLOv8n/YOLO11n that runs >100 FPS full-frame typically drops to roughly 5-15 FPS with a dense grid; on CPU it is sub-1 FPS and not interactive. To run live: make it an opt-in mode (off by default), use a small model (yolo*n), use a coarse 2x2 or 2x3 grid with overlap ~0.2 rather than a fine grid, optionally batch the slices in one model call, and/or trigger slicing only on a region-of-interest or every Nth frame. Single-frame still images are fully real-time-irrelevant. Net: interactive on consumer GPU with a coarse grid; not interactive on CPU.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add a sliced-inference path to src/models/yolo_wrapper.py: add a `sahi: bool = False` (plus `slice_wh=(512,512)`, `overlap=0.2`) flag to YOLODetector.__init__, and in detect() branch to a `_detect_sliced()` that uses SAHI's get_sliced_prediction over the Ultralytics model (`AutoDetectionModel.from_pretrained(model_type='ultralytics', ...)`). Map the returned object-prediction list back into the existing Detection dataclass (bbox/confidence/class_id/class_name) so all downstream consumers — zone counting, line crossing, tracking/ReID, density heatmaps in src/analytics, and the Streamlit dashboard — work unchanged. Add `sahi` to requirements.txt, expose a 'Tiled / small-object mode' toggle plus a frame-stride control in src/dashboard.py, and keep it off by default in src/stream.py to preserve real-time FPS on full-frame mode.
paperCards.limitations: Inference cost scales linearly with the number of slices (one forward pass per patch), so dense grids are far slower than single-pass detection. Slicing removes global context, hurting large objects that exceed one slice (mitigated by adding a full-image pass). Magnifying patches can induce small-object hallucinations / false positives on background. Fixed slice size wastes compute on empty regions when objects are sparse. The big fine-tuning gains require retraining on sliced data, not just inference.

paper.title: SAM 2: Segment Anything in Images and Videos
paper.authors: Ravi, Gabeur, Hu et al. (Meta AI / FAIR)

paperCards.method: SAM 2 generalizes SAM to a Promptable Visual Segmentation (PVS) task over video via a streaming transformer. A hierarchical MAE-pretrained Hiera image encoder runs once per frame; a prompt encoder (points/boxes/masks) and a SAM-style two-way mask decoder produce the per-frame mask. New parts: a memory encoder compressing each predicted mask+frame into a compact memory, a FIFO memory bank of recent and prompted frames, and a memory-attention module conditioning the current frame on those memories. Frames are processed one at a time conditioned only on past memory, so it streams over live video; prompts on any frame propagate forward/backward. Trained jointly on images and the new SA-V dataset via a model-in-the-loop data engine.
paperCards.headlineResults: ~44 FPS real-time video (A100). Variants (SAM 2.1, A100, torch 2.5.1): hiera_tiny 38.9M/91.2 FPS, small 46M/84.8, base_plus 80.8M/64.1, large 224.4M/39.5. Video J&F: SA-V test 76.5-79.5, MOSE val 71.8-74.6, LVOS v2 77.3-80.6. ~3x fewer interactions than prior video methods. Images: 6x faster AND more accurate than SAM (58.9 vs 58.1 mIoU 1-click; up to ~61.4 avg over 23 datasets; 5-click mIoU 81.7 over 37 datasets).
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong on a consumer GPU, weak on CPU. The tiny/small Hiera variants (38.9M/46M params) reach ~85-91 FPS on A100, so a mid-range RTX (3060/4060) should exceed 30 FPS at 1024px for single-object streaming; base_plus is a good accuracy/speed middle. Pure-CPU webcam use is not real-time. For on-device edge, use distilled siblings (EdgeTAM ~16 FPS iPhone 15 Pro Max, ~22x faster than SAM 2; EfficientViT-SAM) or export the encoder to ONNX/TensorRT. Recipe: GPU + sam2.1_t/s, prompt once (click or YOLO box), propagate via streaming memory; downscale input and cap memory length to bound latency.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add src/models/sam2_segmenter.py (a Sam2Segmenter subclassing src/models/base.py) wrapping ultralytics SAM2 (sam2.1_t.pt). Auto-prompt with boxes from src/models/yolo_wrapper.py or text-driven boxes from src/models/open_vocab_detector.py (OWLv2), then stream masks frame-to-frame via SAM 2 memory so masks persist with track IDs from src/tracking. Upgrades zone counting/line crossing and privacy blurring (src/privacy.py) from boxes to pixel-accurate masks, and density heatmaps to mask-area. Surface it in src/dashboard.py as a 'Segment and track' mode; default GPU + sam2.1_t/s, fall back to click-to-segment-only on CPU.
paperCards.limitations: Real-time only on a strong GPU; standard SAM 2 on CPU/mobile is impractical for live use (multi-second/frame in some configs even on RTX 3090). Can lose objects across shot changes, long occlusions, or crowded similar-object scenes; struggles with thin/fine structures and fast motion; tracks one object per memory state (multi-object = multiple passes); memory bank growth adds latency on long streams.

paper.title: Depth Anything V2
paper.authors: Yang, Kang, Zhao, Xu, Feng, Zhao (HKU + TikTok/ByteDance)

paperCards.method: A teacher-student pipeline built on a DINOv2 ViT encoder with a DPT regression head (relative inverse depth). Three key practices: (1) train the largest teacher (ViT-G) ONLY on synthetic images, which have pixel-perfect, sharp depth (no real-label noise); (2) scale up teacher capacity to absorb the synthetic-to-real domain gap; (3) bridge that gap by having the teacher pseudo-label 62M+ unlabeled real images, then train smaller student models (ViT-S/B/L) purely on these pseudo-labels rather than on noisy real ground truth. This combines synthetic sharpness with real-world generalization. Models are released at four scales (24.8M / 97.5M / 335.3M / 1.3B). Separate metric-depth variants are obtained by fine-tuning the relative-depth backbone on Hypersim (indoor) and Virtual KITTI 2 (outdoor). A new DA-2K benchmark of high-resolution, sparse relative-depth annotations on hard/ambiguous regions is introduced for evaluation.
paperCards.headlineResults: Model sizes: Small 24.8M, Base 97.5M, Large 335.3M, Giant 1.3B params. Training data: 595K synthetic labeled images (BlendedMVS, Hypersim, IRS, TartanAir, vKITTI2) + 62M pseudo-labeled real images (ImageNet, LSUN, BDD100K, OpenImages, SA-1B, etc.). Speed/accuracy: "over 10x faster" and more accurate than SD-based Marigold/GeoWizard. On the DA-2K relative-depth benchmark, ViT-S reaches 95.3% accuracy vs 86.8% for Marigold. Reported inference: small model under ~10 ms/image on A100 (PyTorch), and ~98.6 ms TensorRT for batch-4 480x300 on Jetson Orin NX (~50 ms on Orin AGX). Metric fine-tunes: max depth 20 m indoor (Hypersim), 80 m outdoor (Virtual KITTI 2).
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit, and the reason this is the right pick over the metric contenders. The ViT-Small variant is only 24.8M params (Apache-2.0 licensed), the lightest of any candidate. On a consumer/desktop GPU the small model runs comfortably real-time (community TensorRT builds report ~25-72 FPS depending on resolution/precision; sub-10 ms/image on A100). On edge (Jetson Orin NX) TensorRT gives ~98 ms for batch-4 480x300, i.e. roughly real-time per single stream. On a pure laptop CPU the original PyTorch is ~1-3 FPS, but ONNX Runtime + OpenVINO INT8 at 256-384 input pushes the small model to ~6-20 FPS — interactive. For a live webcam: export ViT-S to ONNX (fabio-sim/Depth-Anything-ONNX), letterbox to 384/256, normalize ImageNet stats, run via onnxruntime (OpenVINO/CUDA EP) or TensorRT, upsample the inverse-depth map back to frame size; run depth every Nth frame if pairing with YOLO to protect loop FPS. Output is relative (ordinal) depth; use the Hypersim/vKITTI2 metric checkpoints or single-anchor calibration if meters are required.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add a depth subsystem mirroring the existing detector pattern. Create src/models/depth_estimator.py with a DepthEstimator class subclassing a new BaseDepthEstimator ABC (parallel to BaseDetector in src/models/base.py), wrapping Depth Anything V2-Small via ONNX Runtime (CUDA/OpenVINO EP) with a depth(frame)->np.ndarray method returning a normalized inverse-depth map. Add scripts/run_depth.py as a webcam demo mirroring the existing run_webcam.py, showing side-by-side RGB + INFERNO colormap. Then wire per-detection depth sampling: for each YOLO Detection, sample masked-median depth over a 20%-shrunk bbox with MAD outlier rejection, storing it on the Detection dataclass (extend src/models/base.py with an optional depth field). This unlocks depth-aware zone counting (3D prisms), too-close anomaly alerts, and a depth cost term in the BoT-SORT/ByteTrack association. Update docs/webcam_depth_research.md (already scaffolded) with the V2 benchmark numbers, the ONNX/OpenVINO INT8 recipe, and the metric-calibration plan; log experiments per its Section 8 milestones.
paperCards.limitations: Default outputs are RELATIVE inverse depth (scale/shift-ambiguous), not metric meters — cross-frame absolute thresholds are unreliable without per-frame normalization or calibration; the separate metric checkpoints are domain-specific (indoor Hypersim ≤20 m, outdoor vKITTI2 ≤80 m) and generalize worse than purpose-built metric models like UniDepth/Metric3Dv2. No temporal modeling, so video shows flicker/scale drift (addressed later by Video Depth Anything). Quality scales with model size; the real-time ViT-S trades sharpness for speed. The high-quality Base/Large/Giant weights are CC-BY-NC-4.0 (non-commercial) — only the Small model is Apache-2.0. Synthetic-heavy teacher training can leave residual domain bias on unusual webcam exposure/white-balance; thin/transparent/reflective surfaces remain hard. No camera-intrinsics awareness in the relative model.

paper.title: REACT: Real-time Efficiency and Accuracy Compromise for Tradeoffs in Scene Graph Generation
paper.authors: Neau, Santos, Bosser, Buche, Sugimoto

paperCards.method: REACT is a one-stage, real-time SGG architecture built on the authors' MIT-licensed SGG-Benchmark. Its central design choice is replacing the standard two-stage Faster-RCNN detector with a single-stage YOLOv8 backbone (later YOLO11/YOLO12, plus open-vocabulary YOLO-World), which alone cuts detection latency ~85% (about 2.75x faster) and raises detection accuracy. A lightweight relation head (a PE-NET-style predicate classifier) then predicts relationships over detected entities. Crucially, REACT filters the number of box proposals before relation classification, drastically cutting the quadratic number of candidate object pairs that must be scored. The paper further introduces Dynamic Candidate Selection (DCS), an inference-time method that adaptively prunes candidate pairs to reduce latency by an average 66.5% without hurting accuracy. The result balances detection accuracy, relation accuracy, and speed in one model.
paperCards.headlineResults: REACT achieves the highest inference speed among existing SGG models: 2.7x faster than SOTA while improving object-detection accuracy by 58%. Reported latency is 32.5ms (YOLOv8m configuration), with a real-time configuration reaching ~23ms; Dynamic Candidate Selection (DCS) reduces latency a further 66.5% on average without performance loss. Swapping Faster-RCNN for YOLOv8 gives an 84.99% latency improvement (~2.75x) and +19.4% F1@K. mean Recall is ~mR@K 18.0 (vs 20.7 for the follow-up REACT++). By contrast the prior one-stage transformer RelTR runs at 16.6 FPS with 63.7M params (R@50 27.5, mR@50 10.8). The follow-up REACT++ reaches 35.8M params and 13.4ms (~55 FPS) via ONNX export.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit. REACT is the only candidate explicitly engineered for real-time SGG, and it is built on a YOLOv8 backbone this repo already runs via ultralytics. On a consumer GPU it reports ~23-32.5ms per frame (roughly 30-43 FPS), and the follow-up REACT++ reaches 13.4ms (~55 FPS) with ONNX export, so an interactive 20-30+ FPS webcam stream is realistic on a mid-range GPU. The relation head adds only modest overhead on top of YOLO (REACT++ total ~35.8M params). For a live stream you would run YOLO detection per frame, filter proposals (or use DCS), then score relations on the kept pairs; on CPU expect to drop below interactive rates, so ONNX/TensorRT export and a small YOLO size (n/s) plus aggressive candidate pruning would be needed at the edge.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add a RelationGraphGenerator to src/analytics/scene_understanding.py that upgrades the current heuristic geometric relations (near/above/inside, computed in _find_relations) into a learned scene graph. Reuse the project's existing ultralytics YOLO detections as REACT's detection stage, then attach REACT's lightweight predicate head (load the MIT-licensed maelic/SGG-Benchmark PSG/VG150 ONNX weights via onnxruntime, matching the repo's existing ONNX export path) to classify semantic predicates (holding, riding, sitting-on) over the top-K filtered detection pairs. Populate the existing ObjectRelation.relation field with the predicted predicate (keep geometric relations as a fast fallback when no GPU/weights), feed the resulting triplets into the Streamlit dashboard and the alert system (e.g. trigger on person-holding-knife), and gate it behind a config flag so the CPU heuristic path stays the default.
paperCards.limitations: Accuracy still trails the strongest (slower) two-stage methods on absolute mean-Recall; the follow-up REACT++ notes REACT is not competitive with PE-NET/SQUAT on accuracy. Predicate vocabulary is closed-set and fixed to the training dataset (VG150/IndoorVG/PSG label spaces), unless paired with the open-vocab YOLO-World detector. Reported latencies are GPU figures; CPU-only real-time throughput is not the focus. Like all VG-trained SGG, it inherits long-tailed predicate bias and noisy annotations, and SGG quality is heavily bottlenecked by detector proposal quality. The 23ms vs 32.5ms figures reflect different configurations and should be checked against the exact GPU and YOLO size.

paper.title: AnyCalib: On-Manifold Learning for Model-Agnostic Single-View Camera Calibration
paper.authors: Tirado-Garin & Civera (University of Zaragoza, I3A)

paperCards.method: AnyCalib reframes calibration as dense per-pixel ray regression rather than directly regressing a fixed parameter vector. A DINOv2 ViT-L/14 encoder feeds a lightweight DPT-style decoder that predicts a per-pixel field of viewing directions (a FoV field, an intermediate shown by ablation to beat regressing rays directly). Each ray is treated as a point on the sphere manifold and learned in local Euclidean tangent coordinates via log/exp maps (on-manifold), giving stable model-free targets. From the predicted dense rays, intrinsics for a chosen camera model are recovered in closed form by least-squares fitting, so one trained network serves pinhole, Brown-Conrady radial, Kannala-Brandt fisheye, UCM/EUCM and division models, and even handles cropped/stretched (edited) images. The lightweight decoder trims about 20 percent compute (~187.8 GMACs total) versus a standard DPT head.
paperCards.headlineResults: On Stanford2D3D, AnyCalib (pinhole) reaches 1.13 deg angular error, 2.55 deg vertical-FoV error and vFoV AUC@1/5/10 = 21.2/46.8/64.6, beating GeoCalib (1.45 deg angle, 3.23 deg vFoV, vFoV AUC@1=17.4) and 3D foundation models (DUSt3R, MoGe). The general variant anycalib_gen stays close at 1.30 deg / 2.95 deg vFoV while also handling distorted/fisheye inputs. Authors report consistently outperforming GeoCalib, WildCamera and DroidCalib across Stanford2D3D, MegaDepth, TartanAir and LaMAR despite training on orders of magnitude less data. Compute ~187.8 GMACs; backbone DINOv2 ViT-L/14 (~300M params). No FPS/latency table is published; calibration is a one-shot per-camera step, not per-frame.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Not a per-frame model and does not need to be. The DINOv2 ViT-L/14 backbone (~187.8 GMACs, ~300M params, ~320px input) runs on the order of tens of ms on a consumer GPU (comparable ViT-L DINOv2 forwards are ~40-50 ms on an RTX-class card; far slower on CPU), so it cannot sustain 30 FPS live. But intrinsics are static for a fixed lens, so the right pattern is one-shot calibration: grab one (or a few averaged) frames at startup, run AnyCalib once on GPU, cache the resulting K and distortion coeffs, then reuse them every frame for cheap cv2.undistort / cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap remapping in the hot loop. PyTorch-only dependency (Python >=3.10, optional xformers) fits the existing torch stack. For CPU-only edge boxes, run the one-shot calibration offline or export the encoder via the repo's src/deployment ONNX/TensorRT path; per-frame undistortion is pure OpenCV and trivially real-time.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add src/utils/calibration.py wrapping AnyCalib: a CameraCalibrator.calibrate(frame, cam_model='pinhole'|'radial:2'|'kb:4') returning intrinsics K + distortion coeffs, cached to a per-source JSON sidecar. Wire it into src/multicam/manager.py: add optional intrinsics fields to CameraFeed, run a lazy one-shot calibrate() per camera in MultiCameraManager.start() (GPU once), and apply cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap once + cv2.remap in read_all() so all downstream consumers get undistorted frames. Biggest payoff: replace the heuristic class/confidence matching in find_cross_camera_matches() with geometry-aware association by back-projecting detection centers through each camera's calibrated rays onto a common ground plane (homography), turning guesswork fusion into metric cross-camera matching. Calibrated intrinsics also upgrade src/analytics density heatmaps and zone/line counting to real-world units.
paperCards.limitations: Heavy DINOv2 ViT-L/14 backbone (~187.8 GMACs, ~300M params) means inference is GPU-class and not real-time on CPU; unsuitable per-frame on a webcam stream. Trained on panorama-derived crops (~42k images), so very unusual real lenses or extreme distortion may still need a verification pass. Closed-form intrinsics fit requires choosing the camera-model family up front (separate checkpoints for pinhole vs distorted vs edited). Scope is intrinsics + per-pixel rays only, not scene/extrinsic calibration; no official quantitative latency benchmark is published. Estimates are per single image, so noisy frames are best averaged.

paper.title: Channel-wise Topology Refinement Graph Convolution for Skeleton-Based Action Recognition
paper.authors: Yuxin Chen et al. (Chen, Zhang, Yuan, Li, Deng, Hu)

paperCards.method: CTR-GCN introduces Channel-wise Topology Refinement Graph Convolution (CTR-GC). Instead of one adjacency for all channels, it learns a SHARED topology as a generic prior plus a small CHANNEL-SPECIFIC refinement. Each block: (1) linearly transforms joint features; (2) computes pairwise joint correlations (a distance/correlation function on the transformed features) to produce channel-specific topology offsets; (3) adds these offsets to a shared learnable adjacency, giving every channel its own refined, sample-dependent (dynamic) topology; (4) aggregates joint features under these per-channel topologies. This adds very few parameters over a shared-topology GConv. CTR-GC blocks stack with temporal convolutions into the network; final reported numbers fuse a 4-stream ensemble (joint, bone, joint-motion, bone-motion).
paperCards.headlineResults: 4-stream ensemble accuracy: NTU RGB+D 60 = 92.4% (X-Sub) / 96.8% (X-View); NTU RGB+D 120 = 88.9% (X-Sub) / 90.6% (X-Set); state-of-the-art on NW-UCLA. Single CTR-GC model is very small: ~1.46M parameters and ~1.97 GFLOPs (vs ST-GC 1.22M/~1.65G), and in the ablation a single joint stream reaches 84.9% on NTU120 X-Sub. A third-party latency comparison reports CTR-GCN ~22.84 ms per inference (single sample), i.e. ~40+ FPS on a modern GPU.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit for real-time webcam/edge. The model is tiny (~1.46M params, ~1.97 GFLOPs per stream) and consumes joint coordinates directly — exactly the COCO-17 keypoints YOLOv8-pose already outputs — so no heavy heatmap-volume construction (unlike PoseConv3D's 3D-CNN). On a consumer GPU a single stream runs comfortably at interactive FPS (third-party timing ~23 ms/clip => ~40+ FPS); related GCNs at this scale hit 140-190 FPS at batch 1 on an RTX 4080. A live webcam pipeline needs: a per-track sliding window of ~64 normalized keypoint frames feeding the GCN every N frames, run as a single joint-stream (skip the 4-stream ensemble) for latency, optionally ONNX/TensorRT-exported. CPU/edge real time is plausible for the single stream at reduced channels but is not benchmarked in the paper; ONNX-INT8 or a lighter "fast baseline" variant would help on pure-CPU webcams.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Replace the brittle rule-based heuristics in src/models/pose_detector.py (the ActionClassifier class, lines ~187-327) with a learned CTR-GCN head. Concretely: (1) add src/models/ctrgcn.py wrapping a single-stream CTR-GC network (port the official Uason-Chen/CTR-GCN model or use the PYSKL/MMAction2 implementation, exported to ONNX for opencv/onnxruntime inference consistent with src/deployment/exporter.py). (2) In src/analytics/temporal.py, add a per-track keypoint ring buffer (reuse TemporalBuffer/track_id plumbing) that accumulates ~64 frames of normalized COCO-17 keypoints from PoseResult.keypoints. (3) When a window fills, feed it to the GCN and write the predicted action + confidence back into PoseResult.action/action_confidence, so scripts/run_action.py's existing draw/label/summary loop works unchanged. (4) Extend scripts/run_action.py with a --classifier {rules,ctrgcn} flag and a --window-size arg; keep the rules path as fallback. Map the model's NTU label set down to the repo's action vocabulary (or expose full NTU classes). This upgrades the platform from geometric guesses to a real SOTA temporal action model while staying within its YOLO-pose + ONNX/TensorRT stack.
paperCards.limitations: Headline accuracy needs a 4-stream ensemble (joint/bone/motion), multiplying inference cost ~4x; a single stream is faster but a few points lower. Designed for trimmed, pre-segmented clips and a fixed temporal window, not native online/streaming detection of action onset/offset. Trained/evaluated on 3D skeletons (NTU) or curated 2D (NW-UCLA); accuracy depends heavily on upstream pose quality and is sensitive to noisy/occluded 2D keypoints (a weakness PoseConv3D explicitly targets). Multi-person interaction modeling is limited; per-sample dynamic correlation adds compute vs a purely static graph. Latency, while ~23 ms on GPU, is higher than later "fast baseline" GCNs and is not benchmarked on CPU/edge in the paper.

paper.title: Learning Memory-Guided Normality for Anomaly Detection
paper.authors: Park et al. (Park, Noh, Ham)

paperCards.method: MNAD wraps a U-Net-style encoder-decoder with a learnable memory module holding a small set of prototype items that record diverse normal patterns. Encoder features query the memory via attention; read-out items are concatenated with the query and decoded, so the decoder leans on memorized normal prototypes rather than raw CNN capacity. A memory update scheme refreshes items using normal queries, and two new losses (feature compactness, pulling a query toward its nearest item; feature separateness, pushing apart the two nearest items) make features and prototypes discriminative. Two proxy tasks are supported: future-frame prediction or frame reconstruction. The anomaly score combines prediction/reconstruction error (PSNR) with the distance from a query to its nearest memory item; abnormal frames map poorly to normal prototypes, widening the score gap. No optical flow is used.
paperCards.headlineResults: Frame-level AUC, prediction variant: UCSD Ped2 97.0%, CUHK Avenue 88.5%, ShanghaiTech 70.5%. Reconstruction variant: Ped2 90.2%, Avenue 82.8%, ShanghaiTech 69.8%. The memory module adds roughly +2.7% to +4.0% AUC over the no-memory baseline. Speed: ~67 fps (real-time), faster than optical-flow-based competitors. Trained on a single Nvidia GTX TITAN Xp (~1 h / 15 h / 36 h for Ped2 / Avenue / ShanghaiTech). Built on a compact CNN encoder-decoder plus a small memory bank (a fraction of typical autoencoder size); exact parameter/memory footprint not stated in sources fetched.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit for this platform. The reported ~67 fps was on a 2017-class GPU (GTX TITAN Xp); a small CNN encoder-decoder with a tiny memory bank and no optical flow should comfortably exceed 30 fps on a modern consumer GPU, and the team already exports/benchmarks via ONNX/TensorRT so it can be accelerated further.
