CPU-only real-time is unlikely at full resolution (a few fps at most); on CPU-only edge boxes, run it at low resolution, on grayscale, or every Nth frame. The catch is not inference cost but the per-scene assumption: you must train (or fine-tune) on a short clip of the specific camera's normal footage before live use. Practically: capture ~1-30 min of normal video per webcam, train the prediction model once, ship the .pth plus memory items, and score live frames in a rolling buffer (it needs the preceding frames for future-frame prediction).
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add a second, pixel-level anomaly backend alongside the existing detection-metadata IsolationForest. Create src/analytics/mnad_anomaly.py implementing an MNADAnomalyDetector with the same update()/fit()/check() interface as src/analytics/anomaly_detector.py (so the rest of the app and the Streamlit dashboard are unchanged), wrapping the MNAD prediction encoder-decoder + memory module in torch. Add scripts/train_mnad.py to learn normal patterns from a short clip per source and save weights + memory items, and extend scripts/run_anomaly.py with a --backend {isoforest,mnad} flag plus --mnad-weights. Reuse src/deployment for ONNX/TensorRT export and src/alerts.py to fire on a smoothed PSNR-plus-memory-distance score above a per-camera threshold. This upgrades anomaly detection from "unusual object statistics" to true unsupervised motion/appearance anomalies, while keeping IsolationForest as the zero-training default.
paperCards.limitations: Trained per-scene / per-camera on normal-only footage, so it does not transfer to a new camera or layout without retraining or re-fitting the memory; the baseline number assumes a roughly fixed viewpoint (good for a stationary webcam, poor for a moving one). ShanghaiTech AUC (70.5%) is far below Ped2 (97.0%), showing it weakens on multi-scene / crowded data. The anomaly score is uncalibrated PSNR-plus-memory-distance, needing a per-deployment threshold. It detects appearance/motion deviations, not semantic events, and offers no localization beyond per-pixel error maps. Sources fetched did not state exact parameter count, input resolution, or number of memory items.

paper.title: MiniROAD: Minimal RNN Framework for Online Action Detection
paper.authors: An, Kang, Han, Yang, Kim (Yonsei University, UC Merced, Google Research)

paperCards.method: MiniROAD diagnoses the RNN accuracy gap as a train/inference mismatch: training supervises every timestep equally, but at inference only the latest timestep's prediction is used, so early timesteps (with little accumulated context) inject noisy gradients. The fix is a non-uniform loss weighting that down-weights early timesteps and emphasizes later ones, aligning optimization with how the model is actually queried. The model is deliberately minimal: a single-layer GRU (hidden size 1024) that consumes one precomputed clip feature per frame and emits a per-frame class distribution, carrying all history in its hidden state at constant per-frame cost. Inputs are standard two-stream I3D features (RGB + optical flow), Kinetics-pretrained, matching prior OAD work. No transformer, no long-term memory bank, no sliding window.
paperCards.headlineResults: THUMOS'14: 71.8% mAP (Kinetics two-stream I3D features), matching or beating the best transformer OAD methods; TVSeries: 89.6% mcAP; FineAction: 37.1% mAP (numbers from the official jbistanbul/MiniROAD repo). Efficiency: ~10.5M parameters and ~1.08 GFLOPs vs a comparable transformer baseline at ~12.9M params / ~1.96G (roughly half the compute). The GRU itself runs about 200x faster than prior OAD methods (per-frame model latency) on a single RTX 3090; with optical-flow + I3D feature extraction included, end-to-end "Overall FPS" is much lower and is the real bottleneck.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Excellent fit in principle for a streaming webcam loop: the GRU is single-layer (hidden 1024), ~10.5M params / ~1.08 GFLOPs, ingests one feature vector per frame, keeps O(1) state, and needs no future frames or sliding window, so the temporal head is effectively free even on CPU. The catch is the feature extractor: the paper uses two-stream I3D + optical flow, which is too slow for live CPU/consumer-GPU webcam use. To run on a webcam in real time you would drop optical flow and replace I3D with a lightweight RGB clip encoder (e.g. MobileNetV3/X3D-XS or even reusing per-frame CNN features the YOLO stack already computes), feed those into the GRU, and retrain the GRU head with MiniROAD's non-uniform loss. The GRU + lite backbone is plausibly 30+ FPS on a modern consumer GPU and interactive on CPU; expect some accuracy drop from dropping flow.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add a learned streaming action head to src/analytics/temporal.py. Today that file is purely heuristic/geometric (loitering, abandoned-object, crowd, speed via pixel displacement and durations) with no learned temporal model. Implement a MiniRoadHead class wrapping a single-layer GRU (hidden 1024) that persists its hidden state across frames, mirroring the existing per-frame TemporalBuffer.add() / EventDetector.check() loop. Per frame, extract a lightweight RGB clip embedding (reuse the existing torch backbone or a small X3D/MobileNet), GRU-step it, and emit current-action logits; surface high-confidence transitions as a new EventType.ACTION_RECOGNIZED TemporalEvent so they flow into the existing alert system and Streamlit dashboard. Ship a small trainer in src/training/ that applies MiniROAD's non-uniform per-timestep loss weighting, keeping flow optional/off by default for real-time webcam use.
paperCards.limitations: Per-frame model cost is tiny, but the published pipeline still depends on heavy precomputed two-stream I3D features and optical flow, so the real-time bottleneck is upstream feature extraction, not the GRU; the headline FPS isolates the RNN. Accuracy is reported only with Kinetics-pretrained I3D features (not raw RGB end-to-end), so swapping to a lighter RGB-only backbone would change accuracy. It is frame-level action detection (what action is happening now), not temporal localization with precise start/end boundaries. Trained/evaluated on fixed action vocabularies (THUMOS 20 classes, etc.).

paper.title: Rethinking Counting and Localization in Crowds: A Purely Point-Based Framework
paper.authors: Song et al. (Tencent Youtu)

paperCards.method: P2PNet (Point-to-Point Network) directly predicts a set of point proposals representing head locations, matching the raw dot annotations used for training. A VGG16-bn backbone with an upsampling/feature-fusion neck produces dense anchor points; two parallel branches regress per-point coordinate offsets and a confidence score. During training, predicted points are matched one-to-one to ground-truth points via the Hungarian algorithm using a cost combining pixel distance and confidence; matched points are positives (pulled toward GT, high confidence) and the rest are pushed to low confidence. At inference, points above a confidence threshold are kept — their count is the crowd size and their coordinates are the localizations. The paper also introduces density-Normalized Average Precision (nAP), which normalizes the match-distance tolerance by local crowd density so evaluation is fair across sparse and dense regions.
paperCards.headlineResults: ShanghaiTech Part A MAE ~52.7 / MSE ~85.1; Part B MAE ~6.25 / MSE ~9.9 (best at publication, ~4.8% MAE and ~12.9% MSE reduction vs prior best ADSCNet). UCF-QNRF MAE ~85.3; also strong on UCF-CC-50 and NWPU-Crowd. Backbone VGG16-bn, ~21.8M parameters. Reported real-time on GPU: ~94.5 FPS (~21.8 ms/frame) in third-party efficiency comparisons, far above VGG-density methods (~23-28 FPS) and HRNet-based STEERER (~17 FPS). Localization measured with the new nAP metric (per-dataset nAP roughly 50-65% depending on threshold).
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit. The model is compact (~21.8M params, VGG16-bn) and runs ~94 FPS on a desktop GPU and comfortably 30+ FPS real-time on consumer GPUs (e.g. RTX-class) at typical 720p-1080p webcam frames. On CPU alone expect only a few FPS at full resolution; for a live stream on edge/CPU you would downscale the frame, run inference every Nth frame, and export to ONNX/TensorRT (the repo already has scripts/export_model.py + benchmark.py). Unlike YOLO person detection, P2PNet keeps working in dense scenes (dozens-to-hundreds of heads), so it complements the existing detector rather than replacing it. Practical recipe for the webcam platform: GPU path for live density/count, INT8 TensorRT for edge boxes (Jetson), and frame-skipping + tracker smoothing to stabilize the flickering points.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add a crowd-counting model that emits point predictions and reuse the existing analytics seam. Create src/models/p2pnet_wrapper.py wrapping the official VGG16-bn P2PNet weights and returning the repo's Detection objects with zero-area boxes centered on each head point plus a 'crowd' class and confidence. Add scripts/run_crowd_counter.py mirroring scripts/run_heatmap.py: stream frames, run P2PNet, overlay the live head count, and feed the point Detections straight into the existing HeatmapGenerator (src/analytics/heatmap.py — it already accumulates detection centers) so dense-crowd heatmaps stop undercounting where YOLO fails. The same point Detections also drop into src/analytics/zone_counter.py for true per-zone occupancy in congested areas, and into the alert system (src/alerts.py) for crowd-density thresholds. Wire an ONNX/TensorRT export path via scripts/export_model.py + scripts/benchmark.py for edge throughput.
paperCards.limitations: Trained/evaluated on still images of heads, not video; no temporal consistency, so per-frame points flicker and IDs are not maintained (needs an external tracker for trajectories). Confidence threshold for keeping points must be tuned per scene/density and trades precision vs recall. Performance degrades on very small/blurred far-field heads and under heavy occlusion; the one-to-one Hungarian matching can be sensitive to dense overlapping annotations. Reported FPS is on a server GPU; the paper itself does not provide CPU or edge-device latency, and accuracy is reported on benchmark crops, not arbitrary webcam resolutions/viewpoints.

paper.title: XFeat: Accelerated Features for Lightweight Image Matching
paper.authors: Potje, Cadar, Araujo, Martins, Nascimento (VeRLab / Google)

paperCards.method: XFeat is a compact CNN that, from a single forward pass, predicts three maps at 1/8 resolution: a keypoint heatmap (a parallel branch over 8x8 cells), a dense 64-D descriptor map, and a reliability heatmap. The featherweight backbone keeps spatial resolution high while strictly limiting channels, using aggressive early downsampling and shallow convolutions early, then deeper convolutions (channels roughly tripled as resolution drops) to gain a large receptive field and robustness. Sparse mode matches the compact 64-D descriptors at detected keypoints via mutual nearest neighbors. XFeat* adds an efficient semi-dense mode: it matches coarse 1/8 descriptors, then a lightweight match-refinement module offsets each coarse correspondence toward pixel accuracy without an expensive transformer. It is trained on MegaDepth plus synthetic homographic warps of COCO, yielding less landmark-biased, more generalizable descriptors.
paperCards.headlineResults: Speed (Intel i5-1135G7 laptop CPU, VGA/640x480): sparse XFeat 27.1 FPS, semi-dense XFeat* 19.2 FPS, vs SuperPoint 3.0 FPS and DISK 1.2 FPS — about 9x faster than SuperPoint, and comparable accuracy to DISK at ~16x its speed; up to ~5x faster than competing deep local features overall. GPU: ~1,400 FPS sparse VGA on an RTX 4090, and easily >150 FPS single-batch VGA. Accuracy (relative pose, MegaDepth-1500): AUC@5/10/20 ≈ 42.6 / 56.4 / 67.7, comparable to SuperPoint while much cheaper; it also generalizes to indoor ScanNet-1500 without being trained on it. Compact 64-D descriptors; small enough for embedded deployment.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit for this project's consumer-webcam + edge, no-GPU-required goal. XFeat is the rare learned detector+descriptor that hits real time on a plain laptop CPU: 27.1 FPS sparse / 19.2 FPS semi-dense at VGA on an i5-1135G7 with vanilla PyTorch, and ~1,400 FPS on a desktop GPU, so it keeps up with a 640x480 webcam in real time even CPU-only. Depends only on torch (OpenCV for I/O), Apache-2.0, with community ONNX/TensorRT exports for Jetson/OAK-style edge cameras. To run live: load the pretrained model once, downscale frames to ~VGA, extract features per frame, match to a reference/previous frame with mutual-NN + cv2.findHomography/RANSAC, and budget for CPU NMS/post-processing (thread or batch it). Heavier than ORB but far more robust; on weak edge CPUs, extract every Nth frame and interpolate.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add src/utils/features.py wrapping XFeat (load via torch.hub 'verlab/accelerated_features'; detectAndCompute -> keypoints + 64-D descriptors; match() doing mutual-NN + cv2.findHomography RANSAC) as an OpenCV-style alternative to ORB/SIFT. Wire it into two existing weak spots: (1) src/multicam/manager.py find_cross_camera_matches — replace the class-id + confidence-difference heuristic with real geometric correspondence (estimate an inter-camera homography/fundamental matrix from XFeat matches to confirm two detections are the same physical object across overlapping views); (2) src/tracking/tracker.py _extract_embedding — augment the color-histogram re-ID with XFeat keypoint matching between a track's stored crop and re-entry candidates for far more robust re-identification. It also enables a new src/utils/stabilize.py computing the homography between consecutive src/stream.py frames for camera-motion compensation, stabilizing density heatmaps and cutting false anomaly/line-crossing triggers on shaky feeds.
paperCards.limitations: Accuracy still trails the best heavy semi-dense/dense matchers (LoFTR, ASpanFormer, DISK+LightGlue) at the high-accuracy end; raw mutual-nearest-neighbor matching of the 64-D descriptors needs RANSAC/geometric verification and benefits from a learned matcher (the authors pair it with a "LighterGlue" variant) for best pose results. The headline 27 FPS is measured at VGA with the official PyTorch model on a fairly modern i5; higher resolutions, very low-texture/repetitive scenes, and large illumination extremes still degrade matching, and Python keypoint post-processing/NMS can bottleneck CPU throughput.

paper.title: Pose-Based Fall Detection System: Efficient Monitoring on Standard CPUs
paper.authors: Mali & Jaiswal (Vinayak Mali, Saurabh Jaiswal; IIT Kharagpur)

paperCards.method: MediaPipe Pose landmarks + 6 geometric features + threshold voting over a 20-frame buffer; CPU real-time, sensor-free.
paperCards.headlineResults: On the University of Rzeszow Fall Detection (URFD) dataset and the Multiple Cameras Fall Dataset (MCFD): precision 88.8%, recall 94.1%, F1-score 91.4%, specificity 95.6%. Runs in real time on standard CPUs (no GPU); the pose backbone is MediaPipe Pose / BlazePose (lightweight, few-MB on-device model). The paper emphasizes CPU real-time operation but does not publish an explicit FPS/latency figure or a measured parameter/memory count.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strongly applicable. The whole pipeline is built to run real-time on a standard CPU with no GPU or sensors: MediaPipe Pose / BlazePose is a small on-device model (single-digit MB) that comfortably hits interactive rates (~20-30 FPS) on consumer laptop CPUs, and the fall logic is just per-frame geometry + a 20-frame ring buffer (negligible cost). On a live webcam stream you would run pose per frame, push 6 features into a deque, and trigger after the voting threshold persists. On the target repo's existing YOLOv8n-pose path (already used in pose_detector.py), this runs even faster on a consumer GPU and is fine on CPU; the only edge concern is single-person assumption and occlusion. This is one of the lightest credible fall detectors for webcam/edge deployment.
paperCards.integrationIdea: The repo's src/models/pose_detector.py ActionClassifier already mirrors this paper but in a weaker form: it uses a single crude torso-angle<30 degrees threshold over a 15-frame deque to flag "falling". Upgrade it to the paper's multi-feature + voting scheme: in ActionClassifier add a FallDetector that, per track_id, computes the paper's six features (height/aspect ratio, torso-leg angle, knee-ankle position, head-floor distance from frame-bottom, upper-body alignment, hip movement speed), thresholds each, and accumulates votes over a 20-frame buffer (raise sequence_length 15->20) before emitting a high-confidence "fall" only when the vote count persists. Wire the confirmed fall into src/alerts.py (emit a fall alert with track_id, bbox, and feature snapshot) so it flows into the existing alert system / Streamlit dashboard. This adds debounced, multi-cue fall detection with near-zero added compute on the current pose pipeline.
paperCards.limitations: Threshold + voting parameters were chosen by trial-and-error and may not generalize across camera angles, mounting heights, occlusion, or lighting; the approach is single-person/monocular-geometry oriented and degrades when landmarks are occluded or the subject is far/partially out of frame. Hand-crafted thresholds are less robust than learned classifiers (cf. GCN methods reporting 99%+ accuracy). No explicit FPS, parameter count, or end-to-end latency benchmark is reported, and only two lab/staged datasets are used, so in-the-wild robustness is unproven.

paper.title: YOLO26: Key Architectural Enhancements and Performance Benchmarking for Real-Time Object Detection
paper.authors: Khan et al. (Ultralytics-focused survey/analysis)

paperCards.method: YOLO26 (released Sep 25, 2025; Ultralytics) reworks the detection head to emit a compact, non-redundant prediction set, giving true end-to-end NMS-free inference, and removes Distribution Focal Loss in favor of a lighter, hardware-friendly direct box parameterization. Removing both NMS and DFL lets exported graphs map cleanly to ONNX, TensorRT, CoreML, and TFLite with fewer custom kernels. Training adds ProgLoss (progressive loss balancing) and STAL (Small-Target-Aware Label assignment) to improve small/occluded-object detection and convergence, plus a new MuSGD optimizer (a hybrid of SGD and the Muon optimizer, inspired by LLM training like Kimi K2) for stable convergence. It is a unified multi-task family (detect, segment, pose, OBB, classify) across five scales (n/s/m/l/x), benchmarked on CPU (ONNX) and NVIDIA T4 (TensorRT FP16) and on Jetson edge devices.
paperCards.headlineResults: Across five detection scales YOLO26 reaches 40.9-57.5 mAP(50-95) on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT(FP16) latency. YOLO26n: ~40.9 mAP, 2.4M params, 5.4B FLOPs, 38.9 ms CPU ONNX, 1.7 ms T4; YOLO26x: 57.5 mAP, 55.7M params, 193.9B FLOPs, ~525.8 ms CPU ONNX, 11.8 ms T4. Mid/large scales: YOLO26m above ~53% and YOLO26l above ~55% mAP at lower latency than transformer detectors. Reports up to 43% faster CPU ONNX inference for YOLO26n vs YOLO11n on an Intel Xeon CPU @ 2.00 GHz. Critically for edge: YOLO26 stays stable under INT8 quantization, whereas RT-DETRv3 shows sharp INT8 accuracy drops.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Strong fit. YOLO26n is 2.4M params / 5.4B FLOPs at ~40.9 mAP — small enough for a live webcam stream. On a consumer GPU it is trivially real-time (1.7 ms on T4 ≈ hundreds of FPS); on CPU, ~38.9 ms ONNX on an Intel Xeon @ 2.00 GHz implies roughly 25 FPS single-thread for the nano model, i.e. interactive 640px webcam inference on a modern laptop CPU via ONNXRuntime, and faster with OpenVINO/INT8 on Intel. NMS-free decoding removes a variable post-process cost (steadier per-frame latency for streaming) and the DFL/NMS removal makes INT8 export robust, unlike RT-DETR. To run on a live webcam: export YOLO26n to ONNX (CPU/OpenVINO) or TensorRT-FP16/INT8 (Jetson/discrete GPU) and feed it the existing OpenCV capture loop; n/s scales for real-time, m/l only with GPU or NPU acceleration.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Adopt YOLO26 as a first-class backbone in the existing export/benchmark pipeline. (1) In src/deployment/exporter.py, change the default model from "yolov8n.pt" to "yolo26n.pt" (or add a model-family arg) — the ModelExporter.export() path already calls ultralytics YOLO(...).export(format=...), so NMS-free/DFL-free YOLO26 exports cleanly to onnx/engine/openvino/coreml/tflite/ncnn with no code change. (2) Add an INT8 quantization path: extend ModelExporter.export() with an int8: bool flag passing int8=True (with a calibration data arg) for openvino/tflite/engine, and have scripts/export_model.py expose --int8 — this directly exploits YOLO26's quantization stability. (3) In scripts/benchmark.py / BenchmarkRunner.compare(), add a head-to-head matrix (YOLO26n FP32 vs FP16 vs INT8 vs YOLO11n/YOLOv8n) on CPU(ONNX) and GPU(TensorRT) so the repo can empirically confirm the "~43% faster CPU" and INT8-stability claims on the target hardware before swapping the default detector used by scripts/run_webcam.py.
paperCards.limitations: Most headline numbers are vendor/Ultralytics-reported benchmarks rather than independent third-party reproductions; the paper is largely an architectural overview + benchmarking report, not a from-scratch peer-reviewed method paper, so ablations isolating each component (ProgLoss/STAL/MuSGD) are limited. Exact per-variant CPU ONNX latencies vary with hardware and ONNXRuntime build. NMS-free end-to-end mode can trade a small amount of recall on dense/overlapping scenes versus tuned-NMS pipelines. CPU absolute latencies for larger scales (m/l/x) remain far from real-time without GPU/NPU acceleration.

paper.title: Plug and Play Active Learning for Object Detection
paper.authors: Yang, Huang & Crowley (Chenhongyi Yang, Lichao Huang, Elliot J. Crowley)

paperCards.method: PPAL is a two-stage, detector-agnostic query strategy that touches neither the model architecture nor the training pipeline (hence "plug and play"). Stage 1, Difficulty Calibrated Uncertainty Sampling (DCUS): it computes per-instance uncertainty from the detector's own outputs, but re-weights it by a category-wise difficulty coefficient that fuses classification difficulty and localization difficulty, so hard/rare classes are not drowned out by easy ones; it then shortlists a candidate pool of the most uncertain images. Stage 2, Category Conditioned Matching Similarity (CCMS): it measures image-to-image similarity as an ensemble of matched instance similarities conditioned on category, then runs k-Means++ over the candidate pool to pick a diverse final query batch. The result combines uncertainty (stage 1) and diversity (stage 2) using only off-the-shelf detector predictions. Implemented on MMDetection.
paperCards.headlineResults: Benchmarked on MS-COCO and Pascal VOC with RetinaNet and Faster R-CNN. In the early active-learning rounds (rounds <=3) PPAL outperforms the previous SOTA MI-AOD/MIAL by 3.4 AP on MS-COCO and 2.8 mAP on Pascal VOC, and outperforms prior work by a large margin overall. Setup: COCO initial labeled set = 2% of images, Pascal VOC initial set = 5%. (Context for the regime: on Pascal VOC, MI-AOD reaches ~72.27% mAP using only 20% of labels, ~93% of the 100%-data performance; PPAL pushes this curve higher.) No FPS/latency/params reported -- it is a data-selection algorithm, not a new detector, so it inherits the speed of whatever detector you plug in.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: PPAL is not a real-time inference model and does not change webcam-time speed -- whatever detector you run stays at its native FPS; PPAL only decides WHICH already-captured frames a human should label between training rounds. That fits this repo's edge story: it makes a small on-device YOLO catch up to a much bigger model on YOUR scenes (your office, your camera angle) using a fraction of the hand-labeled frames, which matters precisely because edge models are small and domain-sensitive. Cost is trivial vs training: stage 1 reuses detector outputs you already produce per frame; stage 2 is an offline k-Means++ over a few hundred candidate images, seconds-to-minutes on CPU, run occasionally -- never on the live stream. Practically: buffer webcam frames + their YOLO predictions, run PPAL nightly/weekly to pick ~50-200 frames to label, fine-tune. No GPU needed for the selection step.
paperCards.integrationIdea: The repo's src/training/active_learner.py currently does pure heuristic uncertainty (UncertaintySampler._compute_uncertainty, lines 98-155: confidence-band + class-conflict + frame-edge scores) with NO diversity step and NO class-difficulty calibration -- so it queues redundant near-duplicate frames. Concretely: (1) Add difficulty calibration to _compute_uncertainty -- track a per-class running difficulty coefficient (e.g. from rolling mean confidence + box-stability/IoU jitter across frames via the existing tracker) and multiply each instance's uncertainty by it, so rare/hard classes survive ranking. (2) Add a new diversity pass, e.g. a select_diverse_batch() method (or a src/training/diversity_sampler.py) that, at export_for_labeling() time, embeds each queued frame (mean of detection crop features, or a lightweight backbone) and runs scikit-learn KMeans++ (sklearn.cluster.kmeans_plusplus -- already a project dep) to pick one representative per cluster -- a practical CCMS stand-in. (3) Surface a scripts/run_active_learning.py flag like --strategy ppal --query-size 100 to run uncertainty-shortlist then diversity-select, replacing the current "top-N by score" behavior, and report the chosen frames to the Streamlit dashboard.
paperCards.limitations: No inference-speed/params/memory numbers (it is a selection method, not a detector). Stage 2 (CCMS + k-Means++ over instance-matched similarities) adds an offline clustering cost that grows with candidate-pool size and instances per image -- it runs between training rounds, not per frame, but is heavier than pure uncertainty ranking. Evaluated only on RetinaNet and Faster R-CNN (two-stage / one-stage anchor detectors); not demonstrated on modern YOLOv8/v11 or DETR-style transformers used here. Difficulty coefficients are category-wise, assuming a fixed known label set, which fits poorly with the repo's open-vocabulary (OWLv2) path. A 2026 follow-up (PAL, arXiv:2601.15688) reports PPAL needing ~20.7% more annotations on average than newer reinforced selection, so it is strong-but-not-final SOTA.

paper.title: ODGEN: Domain-specific Object Detection Data Generation with Diffusion Models
paper.authors: Zhu et al. (Jingyuan Zhu, Tsinghua University; co-authors incl. Apple)

paperCards.method: ODGEN is a box-to-image diffusion pipeline. It first domain-adapts a pretrained Stable Diffusion model by fine-tuning its UNet on BOTH cropped foreground objects and full scene images, so it learns the target domain's appearance and per-class look. For layout control it renders synthesized visual prompts (foreground object patches composited at the desired bounding boxes for spatial constraints) paired with object-wise textual descriptions (per-box class text), so each region is conditioned on the right category. Images are generated conditioned on the bbox layout. A trained foreground/background discriminator then audits each generated box: if no synthetic object is detected in a labeled region, that pseudo-label is filtered, cleaning the synthetic annotations. The image+label pairs are mixed with real data to train detectors. Per-object conditioning and domain fine-tuning are what let it generalize to specialized domains where GLIGEN/ControlNet fail.
paperCards.headlineResults: Adding ODGEN synthetic data improves up to +25.3% mAP@.50:.95 on YOLOv5/YOLOv7 across 7 domain-specific Roboflow-100 subsets, the largest gain among compared generative methods (GLIGEN, ControlNet, etc.). On COCO-2014 (general domain) it leads prior methods by up to +5.6% mAP@.50:.95. Protocol: baselines use 200 real images; augmented runs add 5,000 synthetic images (a from-scratch variant trains YOLO on 10k synthetic, validates on 31k real). Built on Stable Diffusion with a fine-tuned UNet. No real-time/FPS numbers are reported (it is an offline data generator, not an inference model); the follow-up FLORA paper notes ODGEN's pipeline assumes enterprise GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA V100) and generation of thousands of samples.
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Not real-time and not an on-device model. ODGEN is a Stable-Diffusion-based offline DATASET FACTORY: each domain needs UNet fine-tuning, then thousands of images are sampled (tens of denoising steps each at ~512px), realistically requiring a V100/A100-class GPU and hours of compute. It cannot run live on a consumer webcam or edge box, and there is no FPS/latency target. The right use for this platform is a one-time/offline data flywheel: generate labeled synthetic images for rare classes the live YOLO model misses (flagged by the existing active-learning module), train/fine-tune a compact YOLO offline, then export to ONNX/TensorRT for the real-time pipeline. The runtime stays the same fast YOLO; only its training data improves. On a consumer GPU (RTX 3090/4090) generation is feasible but slow (minutes for tens of images); CPU-only generation is impractical.
paperCards.integrationIdea: src/training/augmentation.py is misnamed today: its SyntheticGenerator only does classical geometric/photometric/mosaic/cutout transforms. Add a real diffusion backend, e.g. a DiffusionSyntheticGenerator class (or src/training/diffusion_augment.py) wrapping HuggingFace diffusers Stable Diffusion plus a bbox/ControlNet-style spatial condition, implementing ODGEN's two ideas: (1) fine-tune the UNet on cropped foreground crops + full frames captured from the user's own footage, (2) generate images conditioned on synthesized box layouts with per-box class text, then run a foreground/background discriminator filter before writing labels. Reuse the existing export_dataset() YOLO/dataset.yaml writer so output drops straight into the ultralytics training path. Wire the trigger to src/training/active_learner.py: classes/scenes it surfaces as low-confidence become the prompts/boxes ODGEN generates for, closing an offline rare-class data-flywheel that feeds the real-time detector.
paperCards.limitations: ODGEN is a heavy OFFLINE pipeline, not a real-time component: it requires per-domain UNet fine-tuning plus generating thousands of synthetic images, needing enterprise-grade GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA V100) per the FLORA follow-up; not feasible on a webcam/edge device. Generation quality and box-faithfulness still degrade for complex scenes with many classes and dense, occluded objects (authors' own stated limitation). Diffusion sampling is slow (tens of denoising steps per image at SD resolution). Gains are reported only for YOLOv5/YOLOv7 and these specific domains; the efficiency follow-up FLORA reports beating the ODGEN baseline (500 vs 5000 synthetic images, up to +21.3% mAP@.50:.95), indicating ODGEN's sample/compute budget is large.

paper.title: DeepPrivacy2: Towards Realistic Full-Body Anonymization
paper.authors: Hukkelås & Lindseth

paperCards.method: A detect-then-synthesize GAN pipeline. People are first detected; each individual is anonymized recursively, one at a time, so crowded scenes are handled sequentially. Three style-based generators are used: a face generator (256x256) for faces, a CSE-guided full-body generator that conditions on dense-pose (Continuous Surface Embeddings) keypoints to synthesize a plausible body in the correct pose, and an unconditional full-body generator as a fallback when CSE detection fails. The CSE conditioning plus surface-guided sampling keeps the generated person aligned to the original pose/region, so downstream pose and detection signals are largely preserved while identity is replaced. The authors also introduce a new large full-body dataset (FDH) to train the body generator, substantially improving image quality and diversity over DeepPrivacy1.
paperCards.headlineResults: FID 0.56 on FDF (128x128), improving on DeepPrivacy1 (FID 0.68). Datasets introduced: FDF256 (242,031 train / 6,533 val images, 256x256, from YFCC100M) and FDH (Flickr Diverse Humans, ~1.5M full-body images). Speed: ~11.6 FPS for the CSE-guided full-body generator and ~7.9 FPS for the face generator on a single NVIDIA GTX 1080 (8GB); a comparison SG-GAN generator ran ~7.3 FPS. Reported to give the lowest pose-estimation endpoint error (EPE) among anonymization baselines and better body/face pose retention than blurring (though noted as unsuitable for fine hand-pose work).
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Not real-time on edge as-is. The published 7.9-11.6 FPS is on a desktop GTX 1080 and is per-generator throughput — and it scales DOWN with crowd size because each person is synthesized one at a time, so a frame with N people costs roughly N forward passes. On a CPU-only or low-power edge device it would fall to well under 1 FPS. Realistic deployment on a live webcam stream means: (1) running detection at full FPS but invoking the GAN only on tracked, deduplicated identities; (2) a CUDA consumer GPU (the 1080 figures suggest an RTX 30/40-series could approach ~25-30 FPS single-person); (3) processing at reduced resolution / cropping to person ROIs; or (4) ONNX/TensorRT export (this repo already has scripts/export_model.py + benchmark.py) to cut latency. For multi-person real time at the edge it is currently impractical; it fits an "anonymize-and-store" or low-FPS compliance path better than a live overlay.
paperCards.integrationIdea: Add a RealisticAnonymizer class to src/privacy.py alongside the existing PrivacyFilter, wrapping DeepPrivacy2 (pip install of the hukkelas/deep_privacy2 toolbox) behind the same apply(frame, detections) interface so it is a drop-in upgrade from reversible blur to GAN replacement. Drive it from a new mode (e.g. mode="synthesize") and feed it the person/face bboxes the YOLO detector already produces. Crucially, gate GAN calls through the existing multi-object tracker + re-ID so each identity is synthesized once and cached per track rather than every frame, recovering most of the FPS. Expose it in scripts/run_privacy.py via a --mode synthesize flag and a --device cuda guard that warns/falls back to blur when no GPU is present, and add a DeepPrivacy2 row to scripts/benchmark.py to measure on-device FPS before enabling it live.
paperCards.limitations: Inference is slow for real-time/edge (7.9-11.6 FPS on a 1080) and cost scales with the number of people because anonymization is recursive (one person per forward pass). Quality depends on the detector and on CSE dense-pose detection; when CSE fails it drops to a less faithful unconditional generator. Unsuitable for fine hand-pose / precision tasks. Generated humans can show artifacts in unusual poses, occlusion, or heavy crowding, and identity-replacement does not guarantee defeat of every re-identification attack. GAN models are large and GPU-memory hungry, complicating pure-CPU webcam use. Anonymizes humans only (not text, license plates, screens).

paper.title: DINOv3
paper.authors: Simeoni, Vo, Oquab, Bojanowski, Baldassarre et al. (Meta AI / FAIR)

paperCards.method: DINOv3 trains a 6.7B-param ViT (40 layers, 4096-d, 16x16 patches, RoPE, register tokens) purely self-supervised on LVD-1689M (~1.689B images curated from a 17B pool via hierarchical k-means on DINOv2 embeddings), keeping DINO/iBOT self-distillation (image-level + masked patch-level, no labels). Its key idea, Gram Anchoring, periodically regularizes the student's patch-feature Gram matrix (pairwise patch-similarity structure) toward an earlier cleaner reference, restoring sharp locally-consistent dense features over long training without hurting global features. It adds high-resolution adaptation and post-hoc text alignment for zero-shot. The frozen 7B teacher is distilled into ViT (S 21M, S+ 29M, B 86M, L 300M, H+ 840M) and ConvNeXt (T/S/B/L, 29M-198M) students.
paperCards.headlineResults: ViT-7B frozen backbone (no fine-tuning): ImageNet-1k linear-probe ~88.4% top-1; ADE20K semantic seg 55.9 mIoU (~+6 over DINOv2); NYUv2 depth RMSE 0.309 (best reported); COCO detection 66.1 mAP (frozen backbone + Plain-DETR, SOTA). Pretrained on LVD-1689M (~1.689B images). Distilled: ViT-S 21M, ViT-S+ 29M, ViT-B 86M, ViT-L 300M, ViT-H+ 840M; ConvNeXt-T/S/B/L 29M-198M. Proxy latency: DINOv2 ViT-B ~10-12 ms/img @224 in TensorRT FP16 (~80-100 FPS).
paperCards.realtimeWebcamApplicability: Use the distilled small students, not the 7B. ViT-S (21M) and ConvNeXt-Tiny (29M) are webcam-scale frozen feature extractors; patch features already power real-time unsupervised video segmentation on Jetson Orin NX via k-means on patch tokens. On a consumer GPU a ViT-S/16 forward at 224-518px is well within interactive rates (DINOv2 ViT-B ~10-12 ms in TensorRT FP16 as proxy); on CPU it is feasible at low res/FPS but ONNX/TensorRT export (already in this repo) is recommended. The /14->/16 patch change vs DINOv2 yields fewer tokens, helping latency. Practically: load facebook/dinov3-vits16 (or convnext-tiny) via transformers, run frozen, cache embeddings; reserve larger variants for offline/batch.
paperCards.integrationIdea: src/models/base.py today only defines BaseDetector (detect / detect_and_track). Add a sibling FeatureExtractor ABC there (embed(frame)->np.ndarray global vector; embed_dense(frame)->grid of patch features), then implement DINOv3Backbone (e.g. src/models/dinov3_backbone.py) loading facebook/dinov3-vits16 via transformers with a frozen forward. Wire its embeddings into the existing re-identification (more robust tracking), scene anomaly detection (k-NN/Mahalanobis on frozen features), density heatmaps and scene understanding (dense patch features), and the active-learning sampler. Export the small variant through the repo's existing ONNX/TensorRT path for edge.
paperCards.limitations: Custom DINOv3 License (not Apache-2.0 unlike DINOv2); HF weights are gated and derivatives require a 'Built with DINOv3' attribution. The 7B teacher is far too large for edge (SAM3 + DINOv3-ViT-7B exceeds the 16GB envelope of Jetson Orin NX). It is an embedding/representation model, not a detector: outputs features, so detection/segmentation/depth need a task head on top. Pretraining data is largely Instagram-derived web imagery (domain/bias considerations).

gaps: Real-time detection: report frames RT-DETR/RT-DETRv2/v3 and D-FINE/DEIM (the transformer half of the 'two converging families') as background but gives no head-to-head latency/accuracy table vs YOLOv10/YOLO11/YOLO26 on the platform's actual target (CPU/consumer webcam at 640px); the shipped default is still yolov8n.pt, so the 'NMS-free convergence' narrative is not reflected in the codebase or benchmarked.

Multi-object tracking: no treatment of the post-ByteSORT/BoT-SORT generation that the platform should adopt - Deep OC-SORT, OC-SORT, Hybrid-SORT, BoostTrack/BoostTrack++, and especially camera-motion-compensated trackers for a handheld phone feed. The Kalman-filter vs learned-motion (e.g., MotionTrack/transformer association) tradeoff is unaddressed.

Person re-ID: the report's 'appearance cues that reuse detector compute' claim is contradicted by the implementation, which uses a 96-dim BGR color histogram (tracker.py:_extract_embedding) - no OSNet/CLIP-ReID/TransReID/SOLIDER embedding, no occlusion-robust or clothes-changing re-ID, no domain-generalization. This is the single largest research-vs-code gap.

Cross-camera / fusion: find_cross_camera_matches matches only on class_id + a 0.3 confidence-difference window (manager.py:259-272) - there is NO appearance matching, NO epipolar/homography geometry, NO ground-plane/BEV projection, NO global ID assignment. Modern MTMC methods (graph/hierarchical clustering, ReID-gallery association, the AICITY MTMC line) and the 3D-position-from-2D-views goal in idea.md are entirely unrealized.

Small/long-range detection: missing the concrete toolbox - SAHI/tiled inference, slicing-aware NMS, P2 high-res heads, super-resolution-before-detect, and the relevant benchmarks (VisDrone, TinyPerson, DOTA, SODA-D). For a webcam platform answering 'how does accuracy degrade with distance' (a stated research question) this is under-developed.

Promptable segmentation: SAM2 is listed as a domain but there is no segmentation module in src/ and no segment-anything dependency; the accuracy/latency ceiling vs distilled variants (MobileSAM, EfficientSAM, EdgeSAM, FastSAM, SAM2.1) and video object segmentation / memory-bank propagation for streaming are described abstractly without an integration plan against the existing Detection.mask field.

Monocular depth: no depth module or dependency exists. Missing the metric-vs-relative distinction (Depth Anything V2 relative vs Metric3D v2 / UniDepth / Depth Pro metric), temporal consistency for video (no flicker handling), and how depth would feed the 3D-position and small-object goals.

Scene-graph generation: implementation is a hand-coded distance/direction heuristic over 9 hard-coded scene signatures (scene_understanding.py) - no learned SGG (relation/predicate prediction), no open-vocabulary SGG, no panoptic scene graph; the report should flag that the 'scene graph' domain is essentially unimplemented relative to the literature.

VLM scene description: claimed as a covered domain but there is NO LLM/VLM client, API key handling, or caption module anywhere; no discussion of edge-deployable VLMs (SmolVLM2, Qwen2.5-VL-3B, Moondream2, FastVLM/LLaVA-OneVision) vs cloud, latency/cost, or grounding/hallucination control for the accessibility-assistant use case in idea.md.

Camera calibration & geometry: no calibration code (cv2.calibrateCamera/solvePnP/findHomography) despite being a domain; missing intrinsics/extrinsics estimation, lens-distortion correction for webcams, multi-cam extrinsic calibration, and auto-calibration from vanishing points - all prerequisites for the cross-camera 3D goal.

Skeleton-based action recognition: the report names this domain but the platform uses pure rule-based keypoint geometry (pose_detector.py ActionClassifier) - no ST-GCN/2s-AGCN/CTR-GCN/PoseConv3D/skeleton-transformer, no NTU-RGB+D/Kinetics-Skeleton benchmarking, and the temporal model (LSTM/transformer) promised in idea.md is absent.

Video anomaly detection: implementation is IsolationForest over hand-crafted scene descriptors (count/position/grid histogram); missing the modern VAD line - memory-augmented autoencoders, Jigsaw/self-supervised, accuracy on UCSD Ped2/Avenue/ShanghaiTech/UCF-Crime, and open-vocabulary/VLM-based anomaly explanation. Frame-level AUC numbers are not engaged.

Temporal video understanding: temporal.py is rule-based event logic (loiter/abandoned/crowd thresholds); the actual research domain (video-LLMs, long-context streaming understanding, online action detection, TAD) is largely absent, and there is no 'stateful memory' model as idea.md envisions - just deques of bbox centers.

Crowd counting & density estimation: there is NO density-map model (no P2PNet/CSRNet/DM-Count/CLIP-EBC/PET); 'crowd' is detected by counting person tracks past a threshold (temporal.py _check_crowd), which fails exactly in the dense regime the domain exists to solve. The detection-vs-density crossover point is not characterized.

Fall detection: corrected GMDCSA-24 numbers are honored, but the section lacks fall-specific benchmarks beyond one CPU paper (no Le2i, UP-Fall, CAUCAFall, FallAllD), no false-alarm-rate discussion (the key deployment metric for elderly care), and no comparison of pose-based vs wearable-fusion vs RGB-temporal approaches.

Edge deployment: exporter.py covers Ultralytics export formats, but the report's 'joint optimization' claim is not backed by INT8 PTQ/QAT accuracy-drop data, calibration-dataset handling, structured pruning, knowledge distillation, or measured numbers on a real Jetson/Pi/NCNN target - the benchmark harness uses random-noise inputs (exporter.py:197), which does not measure accuracy at all.

Active learning: report should distinguish uncertainty sampling (entropy/margin/least-confidence) vs diversity/core-set vs learning-loss vs BADGE; for detection specifically, box-level vs image-level acquisition and class-imbalance-aware acquisition are the hard parts and likely under-covered.

Generative augmentation: the domain name is contradicted by the code - SyntheticGenerator does only classical OpenCV transforms (flip/rotate/cutout/mosaic/noise), with NO diffusion/SDXL/ControlNet/copy-paste/Cut-Paste-Learn. The central research question ('how much synthetic data can replace real') has no diffusion pipeline to answer it, and layout-to-image / annotation-preserving generation is unaddressed.

Privacy-preserving vision: privacy.py is Gaussian blur/pixelate/blackout on bounding boxes; missing reversible/encryption-based anonymization, face-swap/synthetic-face anonymization that preserves utility, k-same/differential-privacy, federated learning, and the re-identification-attack robustness of naive blur (known to be reversible).

Self-supervised / foundation backbones: DINOv2/DINOv3, SAM image encoder, and EVA-02 are likely named, but the platform-relevant question - amortizing ONE frozen backbone across detection/depth/segmentation/re-ID heads to save webcam compute - is the stated unifying theme yet has no architectural proposal or shared-feature-cache design.
missingTopics: Event / neuromorphic cameras (DVS) and event-RGB fusion - relevant for high-speed motion and low-light, a natural extension of the low-light domain.

Audio-visual perception - microphones are standard on webcams/phones; audio event detection (glass break, scream, gunshot), sound source localization, and AV fusion for anomaly/fall detection are entirely absent.
Hand tracking, gesture recognition, and 3D hand pose (MediaPipe Hands, WiLoR) - core for HCI/accessibility uses the platform targets.
Gaze estimation, eye tracking, and attention - useful for retail analytics, driver monitoring, accessibility.
Face analysis beyond blurring: face detection/recognition/verification (ArcFace/AdaFace), facial landmarks, expression/emotion, age/gender, anti-spoofing/liveness, and deepfake detection.
Tracking/segmentation/detection by natural language: referring expression segmentation/comprehension, GroundingSAM, and language-queried video tracking - a clear evolution of the open-vocab domain.
3D reconstruction / NeRF / 3D Gaussian Splatting / SLAM / structure-from-motion / Vio - the path from multi-cam 2D to a true 3D spatial model that idea.md explicitly wants.
Bird's-eye-view (BEV) perception and ground-plane occupancy mapping - the standard representation for multi-camera fusion and zone analytics.
Trajectory forecasting / motion prediction (Social-GAN, Trajectron++, social-force) - needed to predict collisions, intrusions, and crowd surges proactively.
Optical flow and motion segmentation (RAFT, GMFlow, SEA-RAFT) - foundational temporal primitive useful for anomaly, action, and background modeling.
Re-identification of non-person objects and fine-grained recognition (vehicle re-ID/ANPR/license plates, animal/wildlife re-ID) - directly serves the parking-lot and wildlife use cases listed.
OCR / scene text recognition and document/sign reading - high value for the accessibility-assistant use case.
Multimodal RGB-D / thermal / IR / NIR sensing - thermal is the dominant modality for reliable person detection in darkness and for fever/industrial monitoring.
Adversarial robustness, physical adversarial patches, model security, and backdoor/data-poisoning defenses - a surveillance platform is a security target.
Out-of-distribution / open-set detection and uncertainty calibration - knowing when the model is wrong is critical for safety/anomaly use and is distinct from the IsolationForest scene anomaly.
Continual / online / test-time adaptation and domain adaptation (sim-to-real, lighting/season drift) - cameras run for months; static models degrade.
Streaming/online perception latency models (e.g., streaming-perception / sAP metric) and full end-to-end pipeline latency budgeting (decode-infer-track-render), not just single-op benchmarks.
Video compression / codec-aware CV, ROI encoding, and bandwidth-constrained multi-camera streaming - the systems layer for the 'cheap consumer phones' premise.
MLOps for vision: data/version management, model registry, drift monitoring, A/B evaluation, and dataset curation/dedup at scale.

Ethics, bias, fairness, and regulatory compliance (EU AI Act high-risk biometric rules, surveillance governance) - a glaring omission for a person-tracking/biometric-adjacent platform that only treats privacy as pixel blur.
Synthetic-world / simulation engines for data and digital twins (Unreal/CARLA/Isaac/Kubric, NVIDIA Omniverse) - a more powerful 'synthetic data' route than image-space augmentation.
Multi-object tracking and counting under heavy occlusion via group/crowd-aware tracking - the bridge between the tracking and crowd-counting domains, owned by neither.
weakestFindings: The claim that the literature has 'converged on NMS-free / near-constant-latency inference, appearance cues that reuse detector compute, and TensorRT/ONNX as a first-class target' is asserted as a unifying frame but the platform's own code refutes the appearance-reuse part (color-histogram re-ID) and still defaults to yolov8n.pt - the convergence narrative is over-stated relative to evidence.

YOLO26 is cited as making NMS-free the default and removing DFL 'for cleaner export'; this product/model is bleeding-edge and the authorship was already corrected twice (Sundaram -> Khan -> Sapkota in the notes), signaling the underlying citation is shaky and the technical claims (DFL removal, NMS-free default) deserve primary-source verification rather than restatement.

The fall-detection figures required two rounds of correction (the URFD/MCFD numbers were refuted; only GMDCSA-24 at 86.86% acc survives) - a single small-dataset result is thin evidence for any general claim about CPU-based pose fall detection, and 86.86% accuracy with no false-positive-rate context is near-meaningless for deployment.

Any per-paper accuracy/latency/FPS numbers in the synthesized sections are suspect because the platform's own BenchmarkRunner feeds random-noise images (exporter.py:197) - so internally-generated numbers measure throughput only, never accuracy, and external numbers are not reproduced on the target hardware.

The 'distilled/quantized variant makes the foundation model survivable on a consumer webcam' arc (segmentation, depth) is presented as established, but with no module, dependency, or measured on-device FPS in the repo it is an untested assertion - especially for SAM2/Depth Anything at webcam resolution and frame rate.

Cross-camera fusion claims (the B-cluster's 'what is where in the scene') rest on find_cross_camera_matches, which cannot actually associate identities across views (class+confidence only) - the section likely overstates multi-camera capability the platform does not have.

Crowd-counting coverage is weak-by-construction: with no density model, any stated crowd numbers come from person-track counting, which is known to collapse in dense scenes - claims about crowd estimation accuracy are not supportable by the implementation.

The 'generative / synthetic data augmentation' domain's findings are the weakest in kind: the code labeled 'SyntheticGenerator' contains no generative model, so any conclusion about synthetic-data efficacy is detached from the platform's actual augmentation (classical transforms).
suggestedNextSteps: Replace the color-histogram re-ID in tracker.py with a real lightweight embedding (OSNet-x0.25 or CLIP-ReID distilled) and run an ablation on Market-1501/MSMT17 plus a held-out 'leave-and-reenter' webcam protocol; report rank-1/mAP and per-frame latency on CPU.

Implement geometry-based cross-camera fusion: per-camera homography to a shared ground plane (cv2.findHomography from 4 clicked correspondences), then associate via projected foot-point distance + appearance gallery; assign global IDs and measure IDF1 against a 2-camera (webcam+phone) recording.

Run a real edge benchmark matrix on actual hardware (Jetson Orin Nano + Raspberry Pi 5 + laptop CPU) across PyTorch/ONNX/OpenVINO/TensorRT/NCNN with INT8 PTQ, reporting BOTH mAP (on a real validation set, not random noise) and end-to-end pipeline FPS - fix exporter.py to benchmark with real frames.

Add a promptable-segmentation path: integrate EdgeSAM/MobileSAM/FastVLM-class model behind the existing Detection.mask field, prompt it with detector boxes or text via GroundingDINO, and characterize FPS vs SAM2 at 640px on the target devices.

Integrate a metric monocular depth model (Depth Anything V2 small or Metric3Dv2) to produce per-track distance, then quantitatively answer idea.md's 'accuracy vs distance' question and feed depth into small-object and 3D-position experiments.

Replace the rule-based ActionClassifier with a skeleton GCN/transformer (CTR-GCN or PoseConv3D) trained on NTU-RGB+D or a custom fall set, and compare F1/false-alarm-rate against the current geometric rules on UP-Fall/GMDCSA-24.
Add a density-estimation head (CLIP-EBC or P2PNet) and empirically find the crowd-density crossover where detection-counting fails vs density regression on ShanghaiTech/JHU-Crowd.

Build an actual generative-augmentation pipeline (SDXL + ControlNet or Cut-Paste-Learn copy-paste) with annotation preservation, then run the 'synthetic-to-real ratio' study on a rare class and report mAP vs real-data fraction - the experiment the domain is named for.

Add a VLM scene-description module (SmolVLM2 / Qwen2.5-VL-3B locally, or a cloud Claude/GPT path) with a grounding/verification step against detector outputs to suppress hallucination, and measure caption accuracy + latency for the accessibility use case.

Harden privacy beyond Gaussian blur: add reversible/encrypted-region anonymization and a synthetic-face-swap option, and run a re-identification attack to demonstrate that naive blur leaks identity (motivating the upgrade), aligning with EU AI Act biometric constraints.

Add an audio-visual channel (mic-based event detection + AV fusion) and an event-camera or thermal track as exploratory modalities, since both directly strengthen the low-light and anomaly/fall domains already claimed.

Introduce OOD/open-set detection and uncertainty calibration as a cross-cutting safety layer, and wire its scores into both the active-learning acquisition function and the anomaly detector to replace the brittle IsolationForest-on-handcrafted-features approach.

Add trajectory forecasting (Trajectron++ or a light social-force model) on top of existing tracks to enable proactive zone-intrusion/collision/crowd-surge alerts rather than after-the-fact event detection.

Establish a standing reproducibility harness: pin model versions, cache one frozen DINOv2/SAM-encoder backbone shared across heads, and record every experiment (hypothesis/method/results) in docs/research_log.md as idea.md requires - currently no results log exists.
critique.overallAssessment: The review covers a genuinely broad and well-chosen domain set (24 areas) and the four cluster narratives are coherent, but the effort has a systemic flaw: it conflates 'domain researched' with 'domain implemented,' and several syntheses describe a state of the art the platform does not actually reach. Ground-truthing against the code shows that roughly a third of the claimed domains have NO implementation anchor at all - monocular depth, promptable segmentation (SAM2), camera calibration, crowd density estimation, VLM scene description, and GroundingDINO are research-only with no module or dependency in requirements.txt. Worse, three domains are actively contradicted by the code: re-ID is a 96-dim color histogram (not a deep embedding), 'skeleton-based action recognition' is hand-tuned keypoint geometry (no GCN/transformer), and 'generative/synthetic augmentation' is classical OpenCV transforms with zero generative models despite the class literally being named SyntheticGenerator. Cross-camera 'fusion' matches only on class_id + confidence and cannot associate identities or geometry, and crowd 'counting' is person-track thresholding that fails in exactly the dense regime the domain exists for. The verification process caught real errors (YOLO26 authorship corrected twice; refuted URFD/MCFD fall numbers), which is good, but the recurrence signals the citation layer is fragile and bleeding-edge claims (YOLO26 DFL removal/NMS-free default) need primary sources. Method coverage WITHIN domains is also uneven - the modern tracker generation (OC-SORT/Deep OC-SORT/BoostTrack), tiled small-object inference (SAHI), distilled SAM variants, and metric-vs-relative depth are largely background rather than actionable. Finally, the platform omits entire categories a webcam-CV product must consider - audio-visual, event/thermal cameras, hand/gaze, face analysis and liveness, 3D reconstruction/SLAM/BEV, trajectory forecasting, tracking-by-language, OOD/calibration, adversarial robustness, and notably ethics/bias/EU-AI-Act compliance (privacy is treated only as pixel blur, which is known to be reversible). Net: strong breadth and structure, but the report should explicitly separate 'implemented and benchmarkable' from 'aspirational literature survey,' downgrade the convergence/over-claimed narratives, and prioritize closing the highest-leverage code gaps (deep re-ID, geometric cross-camera fusion, real on-device benchmarking with real images, and a genuine generative pipeline).
